Chapter 1: The Real Number System and Completeness

CORE-001

1 Chapter 1: The Real Number System and Completeness

The real number system forms the foundation upon which all of modern analysis rests. For modelers, understanding the precise construction and properties of the reals is not merely an exercise in mathematical rigor—it provides the theoretical underpinning that guarantees the convergence of iterative algorithms, the existence of solutions to optimization problems, and the validity of limiting processes that appear throughout computational science.

This chapter constructs the real numbers from first principles, establishes their fundamental properties, and explores the profound differences between countable and uncountable infinities. We emphasize throughout how the completeness property of the reals ensures that mathematical models behave as expected, particularly when they involve infinite processes.

1.1 The Inadequacy of Rational Numbers

The rational numbers Q, while dense on the number line, possess a fundamental flaw that renders them insufficient for analysis: they contain “holes” where limits of rational sequences should exist but do not. This incompleteness manifests in various ways that directly impact modeling applications.

Definition 1.1 (Rational Numbers). The set of rational numbers Q consists of all numbers expressible as p/q where p ∈ Z, q ∈ Z, and q ≠ 0. Two rational numbers p/q and r/s are equal if and only if ps = qr.

Consider the sequence of rational numbers defined by the iterative algorithm for computing √2:

# Babylonian method for computing square root of 2
function babylonian_sqrt2(n_iterations)
    x = 1.0  # Initial guess
    sequence = [x]
    
    for i in 1:n_iterations
        x = (x + 2/x) / 2
        push!(sequence, x)
    end
    
    return sequence
end

# Generate the sequence
seq = babylonian_sqrt2(10)
println("Babylonian sequence for √2:")
for (i, x) in enumerate(seq)
    println("x_$i = $x")
end

# Check convergence behavior
println("\nDifferences between consecutive terms:")
for i in 2:length(seq)
    diff = abs(seq[i] - seq[i-1])
    println("x_$(i) - x_$(i-1) = $diff")
end

This sequence exhibits the hallmark of a Cauchy sequence: successive terms become arbitrarily close. Yet within the rational numbers alone, this sequence cannot converge to a rational limit, since √2 is irrational. This fundamental gap necessitates the construction of a complete number system.

1.2 Construction via Dedekind Cuts

One rigorous approach to constructing the real numbers employs Dedekind cuts, which identify real numbers with certain partitions of the rational numbers.

Definition 1.2 (Dedekind Cut). A Dedekind cut is a partition of Q into two non-empty sets A and B such that: 1. A ∪ B = Q and A ∩ B = ∅ 2. If a ∈ A and b ∈ B, then a < b 3. A has no greatest element

The intuitive idea is that each cut (A, B) corresponds to a real number that “separates” the rationals in A from those in B. If the cut corresponds to a rational number r, then A = {q ∈ Q : q < r} and B = {q ∈ Q : q ≥ r}. If the cut corresponds to an irrational number, then neither A nor B contains the “boundary” point.

Example 1.1 (Cut Defining √2). Define A = {q ∈ Q : q < 0 or q² < 2} and B = {q ∈ Q : q > 0 and q² ≥ 2}. This cut defines the irrational number √2.

Verification: We must verify that (A, B) satisfies the three conditions for a Dedekind cut. 1. Clearly A and B are disjoint and A ∪ B = Q by construction. 2. If a ∈ A and b ∈ B, then either a < 0 < b (hence a < b), or both a, b > 0 with a² < 2 ≤ b², which implies a < b. 3. A has no greatest element. Suppose a ∈ A with a > 0 and a² < 2. Choose ε > 0 sufficiently small that (a + ε)² < 2. Then a + ε ∈ A with a + ε > a, contradicting the assumption that a is greatest.

The arithmetic operations on cuts can be defined naturally:

Definition 1.3 (Addition of Cuts). If (A₁, B₁) and (A₂, B₂) are cuts representing real numbers α and β, their sum is the cut (A, B) where A = {a₁ + a₂ : a₁ ∈ A₁, a₂ ∈ A₂}.

1.3 Construction via Cauchy Sequences

An alternative construction identifies real numbers with equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences of rational numbers.

Definition 1.4 (Cauchy Sequence). A sequence (aₙ) in Q is Cauchy if for every ε > 0, there exists N ∈ ℕ such that |aₘ - aₙ| < ε whenever m, n > N.

The fundamental insight is that while not every Cauchy sequence in Q converges to a rational limit, every such sequence should converge to some real number. We construct the reals by considering all Cauchy sequences and identifying those that “should” converge to the same limit.

Definition 1.5 (Equivalence of Cauchy Sequences). Two Cauchy sequences (aₙ) and (bₙ) are equivalent if lim_{n→∞} (aₙ - bₙ) = 0, i.e., for every ε > 0, there exists N such that |aₙ - bₙ| < ε for all n > N.

Definition 1.6 (Real Numbers via Cauchy Sequences). The set of real numbers ℝ is the set of equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences in Q under the equivalence relation defined above.

using LinearAlgebra

# Implementation of rational arithmetic with exact fractions
struct Rational
    num::Int
    den::Int
    
    function Rational(n::Int, d::Int)
        d == 0 && error("Division by zero")
        g = gcd(abs(n), abs(d))
        new(sign(d) * div(n, g), div(abs(d), g))
    end
end

Rational(n::Int) = Rational(n, 1)
+(a::Rational, b::Rational) = Rational(a.num * b.den + b.num * a.den, a.den * b.den)
-(a::Rational, b::Rational) = Rational(a.num * b.den - b.num * a.den, a.den * b.den)
*(a::Rational, b::Rational) = Rational(a.num * b.num, a.den * b.den)
/(a::Rational, b::Rational) = Rational(a.num * b.den, a.den * b.num)
abs(a::Rational) = Rational(abs(a.num), a.den)
convert(::Type{Float64}, a::Rational) = a.num / a.den

# Generate Cauchy sequence converging to √2 using exact rational arithmetic
function rational_sqrt2_sequence(n_terms)
    seq = [Rational(1, 1)]  # Start with 1/1
    
    for i in 2:n_terms
        x_prev = seq[end]
        # Babylonian step: x_{n+1} = (x_n + 2/x_n)/2
        x_next = (x_prev + Rational(2, 1) / x_prev) / Rational(2, 1)
        push!(seq, x_next)
    end
    
    return seq
end

# Verify Cauchy property
function verify_cauchy(seq, tolerance_rational)
    n = length(seq)
    println("Verifying Cauchy property with tolerance $(tolerance_rational):")
    
    for k in 1:min(5, n-1)  # Check last few terms
        for i in (n-k):n, j in (n-k):n
            diff = abs(seq[i] - seq[j])
            println("  |x_$i - x_$j| = $(diff) = $(convert(Float64, diff))")
        end
    end
end

# Generate and analyze the sequence
rational_seq = rational_sqrt2_sequence(10)
println("Rational Cauchy sequence for √2:")
for (i, r) in enumerate(rational_seq[1:min(6, end)])
    println("x_$i = $(r.num)/$(r.den) ≈ $(convert(Float64, r))")
end

verify_cauchy(rational_seq, Rational(1, 1000))

1.4 The Completeness Axiom

The defining property that distinguishes ℝ from Q is completeness, which can be formulated in several equivalent ways.

Definition 1.7 (Upper Bound and Supremum). Let S ⊆ ℝ be non-empty. - An element u ∈ ℝ is an upper bound for S if s ≤ u for all s ∈ S. - If S has an upper bound, S is bounded above. - The supremum (least upper bound) of S, denoted sup S, is an upper bound u such that for any upper bound v of S, we have u ≤ v.

Axiom 1.1 (Completeness Axiom/Least Upper Bound Property). Every non-empty subset of ℝ that is bounded above has a supremum in ℝ.

This axiom captures the intuitive notion that ℝ has “no gaps”—every set that should have a least upper bound actually does have one within ℝ.

Theorem 1.1 (Equivalence of Completeness Formulations). The following are equivalent: 1. The Least Upper Bound Property 2. Every Cauchy sequence in ℝ converges to a limit in ℝ 3. The Nested Interval Property: If [aₙ, bₙ] is a sequence of closed intervals with [aₙ₊₁, bₙ₊₁] ⊆ [aₙ, bₙ] for all n, and lim_{n→∞} (bₙ - aₙ) = 0, then ∩_{n=1}^∞ [aₙ, bₙ] contains exactly one point.

Proof Sketch: We prove (1) ⟹ (2) ⟹ (3) ⟹ (1).

  1. ⟹ (2): Let (xₙ) be Cauchy. The sequence is bounded, so {xₙ : n ∈ ℕ} has a supremum α. Using the Cauchy property, one can show that lim_{n→∞} xₙ = α.

  2. ⟹ (3): Given nested intervals with decreasing lengths, the sequences (aₙ) and (bₙ) are Cauchy and converge to the same limit, which lies in all intervals.

  3. ⟹ (1): Given a bounded set S, construct nested intervals by bisection, keeping those that intersect S. The unique point in the intersection is sup S. □

1.5 The Archimedean Property and Density

The completeness of ℝ implies several fundamental properties that have direct computational implications.

Theorem 1.2 (Archimedean Property). For any real numbers a > 0 and b, there exists a positive integer n such that na > b.

Proof: Suppose not. Then the set S = {na : n ∈ ℕ} is bounded above by b. By completeness, S has a supremum α. Since (n+1)a ∈ S for all n, we have (n+1)a ≤ α, which gives na ≤ α - a. This contradicts α being the least upper bound, since α - a < α is also an upper bound for S. □

This property ensures that real numbers don’t have “infinitely large” or “infinitesimal” elements, which is crucial for numerical analysis.

Theorem 1.3 (Density of Rationals). Between any two distinct real numbers, there exists a rational number.

Proof: Let a < b be real numbers. By the Archimedean property, there exists n ∈ ℕ such that n(b - a) > 1. Consider the set {m ∈ Z : m > na}. This set is non-empty and bounded below, so it has a smallest element m₀. Then m₀ - 1 ≤ na < m₀, which gives a < m₀/n. If m₀/n ≥ b, then 1 ≤ n(b - a) < n(m₀/n - a) ≤ n(b - a), a contradiction. Thus a < m₀/n < b. □

# Demonstration of rational density
function find_rational_between(a::Float64, b::Float64, max_denom::Int = 1000)
    @assert a < b "Need a < b"
    
    # Find rational m/n in (a,b) using continued fraction approach
    for n in 1:max_denom
        m_low = floor(Int, n * a) + 1
        m_high = floor(Int, n * b)
        
        if m_low ≤ m_high
            m = m_low
            rational_val = m / n
            if a < rational_val < b
                return (m, n, rational_val)
            end
        end
    end
    
    error("Could not find rational in interval with denominator ≤ $max_denom")
end

# Example: find rationals between √2 and √3
sqrt2_approx = sqrt(2)
sqrt3_approx = sqrt(3)

println("Finding rational between √2 ≈ $sqrt2_approx and √3 ≈ $sqrt3_approx:")
m, n, r = find_rational_between(sqrt2_approx, sqrt3_approx)
println("Found: $m/$n = $r")
println("Verification: √2 < $r < √3? $(sqrt2_approx < r < sqrt3_approx)")

# Show density by finding increasingly precise rational approximations
println("\nRational density demonstration:")
a, b = 0.0, 1.0
for i in 1:5
    m, n, r = find_rational_between(a, b, 10^i)
    interval_length = b - a
    println("Interval [$a, $b] (length $(interval_length)): rational $m/$n = $r")
    # Narrow the interval around the found rational
    a, b = max(a, r - interval_length/4), min(b, r + interval_length/4)
end

1.6 Countability and Cantor’s Diagonal Argument

One of the most profound discoveries in mathematics is that infinite sets come in different “sizes.” The distinction between countable and uncountable infinities has fundamental implications for analysis and modeling.

Definition 1.8 (Countable Set). A set S is countable if there exists a bijection between S and ℕ (or a finite subset of ℕ). A set that is not countable is uncountable.

Theorem 1.4 (Countability of Rationals). The set Q of rational numbers is countable.

Proof: We exhibit a bijection between Q⁺ (positive rationals) and ℕ using Cantor’s enumeration. List all positive rationals p/q in a grid where row q contains rationals with denominator q. Traverse this grid diagonally, skipping duplicates:

1/1, 1/2, 2/1, 1/3, 2/2, 3/1, 1/4, 2/3, 3/2, 4/1, …

Since we can also handle negative rationals and zero systematically, Q is countable. □

# Cantor's diagonal enumeration of positive rationals
function cantor_rational_enumeration(n_terms::Int)
    rationals = Tuple{Int, Int}[]  # Store as (numerator, denominator)
    seen = Set{Rational}()
    count = 0
    
    # Traverse diagonals: sum of numerator and denominator = k
    k = 2
    while count < n_terms
        # Diagonal k: all (p,q) where p + q = k, p,q ≥ 1
        for p in 1:(k-1)
            q = k - p
            r = Rational(p, q)
            
            if !(r in seen)  # Skip if we've seen this rational value
                push!(seen, r)
                push!(rationals, (p, q))
                count += 1
                
                if count >= n_terms
                    break
                end
            end
        end
        k += 1
    end
    
    return rationals
end

# Generate first 20 positive rationals in Cantor's order
rationals = cantor_rational_enumeration(20)
println("First 20 positive rationals in Cantor's enumeration:")
for (i, (p, q)) in enumerate(rationals)
    r = Rational(p, q)
    println("$i: $p/$q = $(convert(Float64, r))")
end

The situation changes dramatically when we consider the real numbers.

Theorem 1.5 (Uncountability of Reals - Cantor’s Diagonal Argument). The set ℝ is uncountable.

Proof: It suffices to show that the interval (0,1) is uncountable. Suppose, for contradiction, that (0,1) is countable. Then we can list all real numbers in (0,1) as a sequence:

x₁ = 0.a₁₁a₁₂a₁₃a₁₄… x₂ = 0.a₂₁a₂₂a₂₃a₂₄… x₃ = 0.a₃₁a₃₂a₃₃a₃₄… …

where each aᵢⱼ is a decimal digit. Now construct a new decimal y = 0.b₁b₂b₃… where bᵢ = 1 if aᵢᵢ ≠ 1, and bᵢ = 2 if aᵢᵢ = 1.

The number y differs from xᵢ in the i-th decimal place for every i, so y is not in our list. But y ∈ (0,1), contradicting the assumption that our list contained all such numbers. Therefore, (0,1) is uncountable. □

# Illustration of Cantor's diagonal argument
function diagonal_argument_demo()
    # Simulate a "list" of real numbers in (0,1) with their decimal expansions
    # In practice, we can only work with finite approximations
    
    supposed_list = [
        "0.123456789012345...",
        "0.987654321098765...", 
        "0.141592653589793...",
        "0.271828182845904...",
        "0.577215664901532...",
        "0.693147180559945...",
        "0.301029995663981...",
    ]
    
    println("Supposed enumeration of all reals in (0,1):")
    for (i, x) in enumerate(supposed_list)
        println("x_$i = $x")
    end
    
    println("\nConstructing diagonal number:")
    println("Looking at diagonal digits:")
    
    # Extract diagonal digits (positions 3,4,5,6,7,8,9 correspond to 1st,2nd,3rd,... decimal places)
    diagonal_digits = []
    diagonal_positions = [3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9]  # Skip "0." part
    
    for (i, pos) in enumerate(diagonal_positions[1:length(supposed_list)])
        if i <= length(supposed_list) && pos <= length(supposed_list[i])
            digit = supposed_list[i][pos]
            push!(diagonal_digits, digit)
            println("Position $i: diagonal digit is '$digit'")
        end
    end
    
    println("\nConstructing new number by changing each diagonal digit:")
    new_digits = []
    for (i, d) in enumerate(diagonal_digits)
        new_digit = (d == '1') ? '2' : '1'  # Change rule
        push!(new_digits, new_digit)
        println("Position $i: '$d' → '$new_digit'")
    end
    
    new_number = "0." * join(new_digits) * "..."
    println("\nNew number: y = $new_number")
    println("This number differs from x_i in position i for all i,")
    println("so it cannot be in our original list!")
end

diagonal_argument_demo()

1.7 Cardinality of the Continuum

The cardinality of ℝ, denoted |ℝ| or 𝔠 (continuum), represents a fundamentally larger infinity than that of ℕ or Q.

Definition 1.9 (Cardinality). Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if there exists a bijection between them. We write |A| = |B|.

Theorem 1.6 (Cardinality Relationships). We have |ℕ| = |Q| < |ℝ| = |𝒫(ℕ)|, where 𝒫(ℕ) denotes the power set of ℕ.

The proof that |ℝ| = |𝒫(ℕ)| uses the binary expansion of real numbers and establishes a bijection between (0,1) and the set of infinite binary sequences.

Example 1.2 (Cantor Set and Fractional Dimensions). The Cantor set, constructed by iteratively removing middle thirds from intervals, provides an example of an uncountable set with zero Lebesgue measure. Its Hausdorff dimension log(2)/log(3) ≈ 0.631 illustrates how uncountable sets can have “fractional” geometric complexity.

# Construction of the Cantor set
function cantor_set_construction(n_iterations::Int)
    # Start with interval [0,1]
    intervals = [(0.0, 1.0)]
    
    println("Cantor set construction:")
    println("Iteration 0: [0, 1]")
    
    for k in 1:n_iterations
        new_intervals = []
        
        for (a, b) in intervals
            # Remove middle third: keep [a, a + (b-a)/3] and [a + 2(b-a)/3, b]
            left_end = a + (b - a) / 3
            right_start = a + 2 * (b - a) / 3
            
            push!(new_intervals, (a, left_end))
            push!(new_intervals, (right_start, b))
        end
        
        intervals = new_intervals
        total_length = sum(b - a for (a, b) in intervals)
        
        println("Iteration $k: $(length(intervals)) intervals, total length = $total_length")
        if k <= 3  # Show first few iterations explicitly
            for (i, (a, b)) in enumerate(intervals)
                println("  [$a, $b]")
            end
        end
    end
    
    return intervals
end

# Demonstrate Cantor set properties
cantor_intervals = cantor_set_construction(6)

println("\nCantor set properties:")
println("- Number of intervals after 6 iterations: $(length(cantor_intervals))")
println("- Total length: $(sum(b - a for (a, b) in cantor_intervals))")
println("- Theoretical limit of total length: 0")
println("- Cardinality: uncountable (same as ℝ)")
println("- Hausdorff dimension: $(log(2)/log(3)) ≈ $(round(log(2)/log(3), digits=4))")

1.8 Computational Implications of Completeness

The completeness of ℝ has profound implications for numerical computation and modeling. It guarantees the existence of limits for many iterative processes, ensuring that algorithms terminate with meaningful results.

Example 1.3 (Newton’s Method and Completeness). Consider Newton’s method for finding roots of f(x) = x² - 2:

The iteration xₙ₊₁ = (xₙ + 2/xₙ)/2 generates a Cauchy sequence that converges to √2. Without completeness, this convergence could not be guaranteed within our number system.

using Plots

# Newton's method with convergence analysis
function newton_sqrt2_analysis(x0::Float64, n_iterations::Int)
    sequence = [x0]
    errors = [abs(x0 - sqrt(2))]
    
    x = x0
    for i in 1:n_iterations
        x_new = (x + 2/x) / 2
        push!(sequence, x_new)
        push!(errors, abs(x_new - sqrt(2)))
        x = x_new
    end
    
    return sequence, errors
end

# Analyze convergence from different starting points
starting_points = [0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0]
n_iter = 10

println("Newton's method convergence analysis:")
println("Target: √2 = $(sqrt(2))")
println()

for x0 in starting_points
    seq, errs = newton_sqrt2_analysis(x0, n_iter)
    
    println("Starting from x₀ = $x0:")
    for i in 1:min(6, length(seq))
        println("  x_$(i-1) = $(seq[i]), error = $(errs[i])")
    end
    
    # Check quadratic convergence
    if length(errs) >= 4
        ratio1 = errs[4] / (errs[3])^2
        ratio2 = errs[5] / (errs[4])^2
        println("  Quadratic convergence ratios: $(ratio1), $(ratio2)")
    end
    println()
end

Example 1.4 (Fixed Point Iterations). Many modeling applications rely on fixed point iterations of the form xₙ₊₁ = g(xₙ). The completeness of ℝ, combined with contraction mapping theorems (Chapter 10), guarantees convergence when g satisfies appropriate conditions.

# Fixed point iteration examples
function fixed_point_iteration(g, x0, n_iterations; tolerance=1e-10)
    x = x0
    sequence = [x]
    
    for i in 1:n_iterations
        x_new = g(x)
        push!(sequence, x_new)
        
        if abs(x_new - x) < tolerance
            println("Converged after $i iterations")
            break
        end
        x = x_new
    end
    
    return sequence
end

# Example 1: x = cos(x) (fixed point around 0.739)
g1(x) = cos(x)
seq1 = fixed_point_iteration(g1, 1.0, 20)
println("Fixed point of cos(x):")
println("Final value: $(seq1[end])")
println("Verification: cos($(seq1[end])) = $(cos(seq1[end]))")
println()

# Example 2: x = (x + 2/x)/2 (equivalent to Newton for √2)
g2(x) = (x + 2/x)/2
seq2 = fixed_point_iteration(g2, 1.0, 10)
println("Fixed point of (x + 2/x)/2:")
println("Final value: $(seq2[end])")
println("√2 = $(sqrt(2))")
println("Error: $(abs(seq2[end] - sqrt(2)))")

1.9 Exercises

Exercise 1.1 (Computational). Implement a function that constructs rational Cauchy sequences converging to any given algebraic number α (solution to a polynomial equation with integer coefficients). Test your implementation with α = ∛2 and α = (1 + √5)/2 (golden ratio).

Exercise 1.2 (Theoretical). Prove that the set of algebraic numbers is countable. [Hint: Each polynomial has finitely many roots, and there are countably many polynomials with integer coefficients.]

Exercise 1.3 (Computational). Write a Julia function implementing the bisection method that uses the nested interval property to find roots of continuous functions. Apply it to find the positive root of x³ - 2x - 5 = 0.

Exercise 1.4 (Theoretical). Prove that if A and B are countable sets, then A × B is countable. Extend this to show that Qⁿ is countable for any n ∈ ℕ.

Exercise 1.5 (Theoretical). Show that the set of irrational numbers is uncountable. [Hint: Use the fact that ℝ = Q ∪ (ℝ  Q) and properties of countable unions.]

Exercise 1.6 (Computational). Implement Cantor’s pairing function π(m,n) = ((m+n)(m+n+1))/2 + n that establishes a bijection between ℕ × ℕ and ℕ. Verify that your implementation correctly inverts the pairing.

Exercise 1.7 (Theoretical). Let S be the set of all finite binary strings. Show that S is countable but the set of all infinite binary sequences is uncountable.

Exercise 1.8 (Computational). The Babylonian method for computing √n uses the iteration xₖ₊₁ = (xₖ + n/xₖ)/2. Prove that this sequence is decreasing for k ≥ 1 and bounded below, hence convergent. Implement this algorithm and verify the theoretical convergence rate.

Exercise 1.9 (Theoretical). Prove the equivalence of the following statements: (a) ℝ satisfies the least upper bound property (b) Every monotone bounded sequence in ℝ converges (c) ℝ satisfies the greatest lower bound property

Exercise 1.10 (Advanced). Show that there exists a bijection between ℝ and ℝⁿ for any n ≥ 2, establishing that these sets have the same cardinality. [Hint: Use the decimal expansion and interleaving of digits, being careful about uniqueness issues.]

1.10 Connections

The completeness property established in this chapter forms the foundation for all subsequent development in real analysis. Chapter 2 will generalize the notion of distance and completeness to arbitrary metric spaces, providing the framework for analyzing convergence in function spaces. The density properties of Q in ℝ connect to approximation theory and numerical analysis, while the uncountability of ℝ has profound implications for measure theory (Chapter 8) and the construction of non-measurable sets.

The computational examples in this chapter illustrate how theoretical completeness guarantees practical algorithm convergence—a theme that will recur throughout the modeling applications in Chapters 6-10. Understanding cardinality differences becomes crucial when working with function spaces (Chapter 9) and understanding which infinite-dimensional spaces can be effectively parameterized for computation.

1.11 References

Apostol, T. M. (1974). Mathematical Analysis, 2nd edition. Addison-Wesley.

Rudin, W. (1976). Principles of Mathematical Analysis, 3rd edition. McGraw-Hill.

Royden, H. L. and Fitzpatrick, P. M. (2010). Real Analysis, 4th edition. Prentice Hall.

Bartle, R. G. and Sherbert, D. R. (2011). Introduction to Real Analysis, 4th edition. Wiley.

Folland, G. B. (1999). Real Analysis: Modern Techniques and Their Applications, 2nd edition. Wiley.

2 Chapter 2: Metric Spaces: The Language of Proximity

In modeling phenomena across science and engineering, we often need to quantify how “close” two objects are to each other. These objects might be numbers, vectors, functions, probability distributions, or even more abstract mathematical entities. The theory of metric spaces provides the unifying framework for discussing proximity, convergence, and continuity in a general setting that encompasses all these contexts.

The metric space formalism is not merely an abstract generalization—it is an essential tool for the modeler. When we calibrate a model to data, we need metrics to measure the goodness of fit. When we study the convergence of numerical algorithms, we need metrics to quantify approximation error. When we analyze the stability of dynamical systems, we need metrics to measure how trajectories diverge. This chapter develops the metric space theory that underlies all such applications.

2.1 Definition and Fundamental Examples

Definition 2.1 (Metric Space). A metric space is a pair (X, d) where X is a non-empty set and d: X × X → [0, ∞) is a function called a metric (or distance function) satisfying: 1. d(x, y) = 0 if and only if x = y (identity of indiscernibles) 2. d(x, y) = d(y, x) for all x, y ∈ X (symmetry) 3. d(x, z) ≤ d(x, y) + d(y, z) for all x, y, z ∈ X (triangle inequality)

The triangle inequality captures the intuitive notion that the direct path between two points should be no longer than any detour through a third point. This property is crucial for many theoretical results and ensures that metrics behave as we expect distance functions to behave.

2.1.1 Euclidean Space

The most familiar metric space is Euclidean space ℝⁿ with the Euclidean metric:

d₂(x, y) = (∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ (xᵢ - yᵢ)²)^(1/2)

This is the ordinary distance we measure with rulers. However, Euclidean space admits many other useful metrics:

  • The taxicab (Manhattan) metric: d₁(x, y) = ∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ |xᵢ - yᵢ|
  • The supremum (∞-norm) metric: d∞(x, y) = maxᵢ |xᵢ - yᵢ|

More generally, for any p ≥ 1, the p-metric is defined as:

dₚ(x, y) = (∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ |xᵢ - yᵢ|ᵖ)^(1/p)

using LinearAlgebra

# Define various metrics on R^n
function euclidean_metric(x, y)
    return norm(x - y, 2)
end

function manhattan_metric(x, y)
    return norm(x - y, 1)
end

function supremum_metric(x, y)
    return norm(x - y, Inf)
end

function p_metric(x, y, p)
    return norm(x - y, p)
end

# Example: Compare different metrics on R^2
x = [1.0, 2.0]
y = [4.0, 6.0]

println("Euclidean distance: ", euclidean_metric(x, y))
println("Manhattan distance: ", manhattan_metric(x, y))
println("Supremum distance: ", supremum_metric(x, y))
println("3-metric distance: ", p_metric(x, y, 3))

2.1.2 Function Spaces

Function spaces are metric spaces where the points are functions rather than vectors. These spaces are fundamental in modeling because they allow us to quantify how different two models (represented as functions) are from each other.

Definition 2.2 (Supremum Norm). Let C[a, b] denote the space of continuous functions on the closed interval [a, b]. The supremum norm is defined as: ‖f‖∞ = sup{|f(x)| : x ∈ [a, b]}

The corresponding metric is d∞(f, g) = ‖f - g‖∞.

The supremum norm measures the maximum deviation between two functions over their entire domain. This is particularly relevant for model validation, where we want to bound the worst-case error.

Definition 2.3 (Lᵖ Norms). For 1 ≤ p < ∞ and functions f, g integrable on [a, b], the Lᵖ metric is: dₚ(f, g) = (∫ₐᵇ |f(x) - g(x)|ᵖ dx)^(1/p)

The L² metric corresponds to the root-mean-square error, which is ubiquitous in statistical modeling and optimization.

using QuadGK

# Define L^p metrics for functions on [a,b]
function lp_metric(f, g, p, a, b)
    integrand(x) = abs(f(x) - g(x))^p
    integral, _ = quadgk(integrand, a, b)
    return integral^(1/p)
end

function sup_metric(f, g, a, b; n_samples=1000)
    # Approximate supremum by sampling
    x_vals = range(a, b, length=n_samples)
    return maximum(abs(f(x) - g(x)) for x in x_vals)
end

# Example: Compare sine and cosine functions
f(x) = sin(x)
g(x) = cos(x)

println("L¹ distance between sin and cos on [0,π]: ", 
        lp_metric(f, g, 1, 0, π))
println("L² distance between sin and cos on [0,π]: ", 
        lp_metric(f, g, 2, 0, π))
println("L∞ distance between sin and cos on [0,π]: ", 
        sup_metric(f, g, 0, π))

2.1.3 Discrete and Combinatorial Metrics

In discrete modeling contexts, we often work with finite sets equipped with specialized metrics.

Definition 2.4 (Discrete Metric). On any set X, the discrete metric is defined as: d(x, y) = {0 if x = y, 1 if x ≠ y}

Definition 2.5 (Hamming Distance). For strings or sequences of equal length n over an alphabet Σ, the Hamming distance is the number of positions in which the sequences differ: dₕ(x, y) = |{i : xᵢ ≠ yᵢ}|

The Hamming distance is crucial in coding theory, bioinformatics (for comparing DNA sequences), and discrete optimization.

# Hamming distance implementation
function hamming_distance(x, y)
    length(x) != length(y) && throw(ArgumentError("Sequences must have equal length"))
    return sum(x[i] != y[i] for i in 1:length(x))
end

# Example: DNA sequence comparison
seq1 = "ATCGATCG"
seq2 = "ATCGTTCG"
println("Hamming distance between DNA sequences: ", 
        hamming_distance(seq1, seq2))

# Example: Binary string comparison
bin1 = [1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0]
bin2 = [1, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0]
println("Hamming distance between binary strings: ", 
        hamming_distance(bin1, bin2))

2.2 Open Sets, Closed Sets, and Topology

The metric structure on a space X induces a natural notion of “nearness” that allows us to define open and closed sets. These concepts are fundamental for understanding continuity and convergence.

Definition 2.6 (Open Ball). In a metric space (X, d), the open ball of radius r > 0 centered at x₀ ∈ X is: Bᵣ(x₀) = {x ∈ X : d(x, x₀) < r}

Definition 2.7 (Open Set). A subset U ⊆ X is open if for every x ∈ U, there exists r > 0 such that Bᵣ(x) ⊆ U.

Intuitively, a set is open if every point in the set has some “breathing room”—a small neighborhood that is entirely contained in the set.

Definition 2.8 (Closed Set). A subset F ⊆ X is closed if its complement X  F is open.

Theorem 2.1 (Properties of Open and Closed Sets). In any metric space (X, d): 1. The empty set ∅ and the whole space X are both open and closed 2. Arbitrary unions of open sets are open 3. Finite intersections of open sets are open 4. Arbitrary intersections of closed sets are closed 5. Finite unions of closed sets are closed

Proof. We prove property (2); the others follow similarly. Let {Uα} be a collection of open sets, and let U = ⋃α Uα. If x ∈ U, then x ∈ Uα for some α. Since Uα is open, there exists r > 0 such that Bᵣ(x) ⊆ Uα ⊆ U. Therefore U is open. □

2.2.1 Interior, Closure, and Boundary

Definition 2.9 (Interior). The interior of a set A ⊆ X is the largest open set contained in A: int(A) = ⋃{U : U is open and U ⊆ A}

Definition 2.10 (Closure). The closure of a set A ⊆ X is the smallest closed set containing A: cl(A) = ⋂{F : F is closed and A ⊆ F}

Definition 2.11 (Boundary). The boundary of a set A ⊆ X is: ∂A = cl(A)  int(A)

Example 2.1 (Intervals in ℝ). Consider the half-open interval A = [0, 1) in ℝ with the usual metric. - int(A) = (0, 1): every point except the endpoints has a neighborhood contained in A - cl(A) = [0, 1]: we must include the limit point 1 - ∂A = {0, 1}: the endpoints are boundary points

# Visualize open balls in different metrics
using Plots

function plot_unit_balls()
    # Create points for unit balls in different metrics
    θ = range(0, 2π, length=100)
    
    # L2 ball (circle)
    x2 = cos.(θ)
    y2 = sin.(θ)
    
    # L1 ball (diamond)
    t = range(-1, 1, length=25)
    x1 = [t; ones(25); reverse(-t); -ones(25)]
    y1 = [ones(25); reverse(-t); -ones(25); t]
    
    # L∞ ball (square)
    x_inf = [-1, 1, 1, -1, -1]
    y_inf = [-1, -1, 1, 1, -1]
    
    plot(x2, y2, label="L² ball", linewidth=2)
    plot!(x1, y1, label="L¹ ball", linewidth=2)
    plot!(x_inf, y_inf, label="L∞ ball", linewidth=2)
    xlabel!("x₁")
    ylabel!("x₂")
    title!("Unit Balls in Different Metrics")
    aspect_ratio!(1)
end

plot_unit_balls()

2.3 Convergence in Metric Spaces

The metric structure allows us to define convergence of sequences in a natural way that generalizes the familiar notion from ℝ.

Definition 2.12 (Convergent Sequence). A sequence (xₙ) in a metric space (X, d) converges to x ∈ X if for every ε > 0, there exists N ∈ ℕ such that d(xₙ, x) < ε for all n ≥ N.

We write xₙ → x or limₙ→∞ xₙ = x.

Theorem 2.2 (Uniqueness of Limits). If a sequence in a metric space converges, then its limit is unique.

Proof. Suppose xₙ → x and xₙ → y. For any ε > 0, there exist N₁, N₂ such that d(xₙ, x) < ε/2 for n ≥ N₁ and d(xₙ, y) < ε/2 for n ≥ N₂. Let N = max(N₁, N₂). Then for n ≥ N:

d(x, y) ≤ d(x, xₙ) + d(xₙ, y) < ε/2 + ε/2 = ε

Since ε was arbitrary and d(x, y) ≥ 0, we must have d(x, y) = 0, hence x = y. □

Example 2.2 (Convergence in Function Spaces). Consider the sequence of functions fₙ(x) = xⁿ on [0, 1].

In the supremum norm: ‖fₙ - f‖∞ = supₓ∈[₀,₁] |xⁿ - f(x)| - If f(x) = 0 for all x, then ‖fₙ‖∞ = 1 for all n, so fₙ ↛ 0 - The sequence does not converge in the supremum norm

In the L² norm: ‖fₙ - 0‖₂ = (∫₀¹ x²ⁿ dx)^(1/2) = (1/(2n+1))^(1/2) → 0 - The sequence converges to the zero function in L²

This example illustrates that convergence depends critically on the choice of metric.

# Illustrate different types of convergence
using Plots

function demonstrate_convergence()
    x = range(0, 1, length=200)
    p = plot()
    
    # Plot several functions x^n
    for n in [1, 2, 5, 10, 20]
        y = x.^n
        plot!(p, x, y, label="x^$n", alpha=0.7)
    end
    
    # Add the "limit" function (discontinuous jump at x=1)
    plot!(p, [0, 0.999, 1], [0, 0, 1], 
          label="pointwise limit", linewidth=3, color=:black, linestyle=:dash)
    
    xlabel!("x")
    ylabel!("f(x)")
    title!("Sequence x^n on [0,1]")
    
    return p
end

demonstrate_convergence()

2.4 Cauchy Sequences and Completeness

Not all sequences that “should” converge actually have limits in their metric space. The concept of Cauchy sequences allows us to identify sequences that would converge if we had enough points in our space.

Definition 2.13 (Cauchy Sequence). A sequence (xₙ) in a metric space (X, d) is Cauchy if for every ε > 0, there exists N ∈ ℕ such that d(xₘ, xₙ) < ε for all m, n ≥ N.

Theorem 2.3. Every convergent sequence is Cauchy.

Proof. Let xₙ → x. Given ε > 0, choose N such that d(xₙ, x) < ε/2 for n ≥ N. Then for m, n ≥ N: d(xₘ, xₙ) ≤ d(xₘ, x) + d(x, xₙ) < ε/2 + ε/2 = ε □

The converse is not always true—some metric spaces contain Cauchy sequences that do not converge to any point in the space.

Definition 2.14 (Complete Metric Space). A metric space (X, d) is complete if every Cauchy sequence in X converges to a point in X.

Theorem 2.4 (Completeness of ℝⁿ). The space ℝⁿ with any p-metric is complete.

Example 2.3 (Incomplete Space). Consider X = (0, 1) with the usual metric inherited from ℝ. The sequence xₙ = 1/n is Cauchy in X, but its limit 0 is not in X. Therefore X is not complete.

2.4.1 The Completion of a Metric Space

Every metric space can be “completed” by adding the limits of all Cauchy sequences.

Theorem 2.5 (Completion Theorem). For any metric space (X, d), there exists a complete metric space (X̂, d̂) and an isometry φ: X → X̂ such that φ(X) is dense in X̂. Moreover, X̂ is unique up to isometry.

The completion X̂ can be constructed as the space of equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences in X, where two sequences are equivalent if their distance goes to zero.

# Demonstrate incompleteness with rational numbers
using Rational

function sqrt2_approximation(n)
    # Newton's method approximation to √2 using rationals
    x = Rational(3, 2)  # Initial guess
    for _ in 1:n
        x = (x + 2//x) // 2
    end
    return x
end

# Generate sequence of rational approximations to √2
approximations = [sqrt2_approximation(n) for n in 1:10]

println("Rational approximations to √2:")
for (i, approx) in enumerate(approximations)
    println("n=$i: $approx = $(float(approx))")
end

# This sequence is Cauchy in Q but converges to √2 ∉ Q
println("\nDistances between consecutive terms:")
for i in 1:length(approximations)-1
    dist = abs(float(approximations[i+1] - approximations[i]))
    println("d(x_$(i+1), x_$i) = $dist")
end

2.5 Equivalent Metrics and Topological Equivalence

Different metrics on the same set can induce the same notion of convergence and the same open sets. Understanding when this occurs is crucial for choosing appropriate metrics in applications.

Definition 2.15 (Equivalent Metrics). Two metrics d₁ and d₂ on a set X are equivalent if they induce the same topology—that is, if a sequence converges in (X, d₁) if and only if it converges in (X, d₂) to the same limit.

Theorem 2.6 (Characterization of Equivalent Metrics). Two metrics d₁ and d₂ on X are equivalent if and only if there exist constants c, C > 0 such that: cd₁(x, y) ≤ d₂(x, y) ≤ Cd₁(x, y) for all x, y ∈ X

Proof. (⇐) Suppose the inequalities hold. If xₙ → x in (X, d₁), then d₁(xₙ, x) → 0, so cd₁(xₙ, x) ≤ d₂(xₙ, x) → 0, hence xₙ → x in (X, d₂). The reverse implication follows by symmetry.

(⇒) The proof of the converse direction requires more careful analysis of the topology induced by each metric and is omitted. □

Example 2.4 (Equivalent Norms on ℝⁿ). All p-norms on ℝⁿ are equivalent. Specifically: - ‖x‖∞ ≤ ‖x‖₂ ≤ √n ‖x‖∞ - ‖x‖₂ ≤ ‖x‖₁ ≤ √n ‖x‖₂ - ‖x‖∞ ≤ ‖x‖₁ ≤ n ‖x‖∞

This equivalence is crucial for numerical analysis—convergence of algorithms can be analyzed using whichever norm is most convenient.

# Verify norm equivalences numerically
using Random
Random.seed!(42)

function test_norm_equivalence(n_trials=1000, dim=10)
    ratios_21 = Float64[]  # ||x||₂ / ||x||₁
    ratios_inf2 = Float64[] # ||x||∞ / ||x||₂
    ratios_1inf = Float64[] # ||x||₁ / ||x||∞
    
    for _ in 1:n_trials
        x = randn(dim)
        push!(ratios_21, norm(x, 2) / norm(x, 1))
        push!(ratios_inf2, norm(x, Inf) / norm(x, 2))
        push!(ratios_1inf, norm(x, 1) / norm(x, Inf))
    end
    
    println("Norm ratios for random vectors in ℝ^$dim:")
    println("||x||₂/||x||₁: min = $(minimum(ratios_21):.3f), max = $(maximum(ratios_21):.3f)")
    println("Theoretical bounds: [1/√$dim, 1] = [$(1/sqrt(dim):.3f), 1.000]")
    println()
    println("||x||∞/||x||₂: min = $(minimum(ratios_inf2):.3f), max = $(maximum(ratios_inf2):.3f)")
    println("Theoretical bounds: [1/√$dim, 1] = [$(1/sqrt(dim):.3f), 1.000]")
    println()
    println("||x||₁/||x||∞: min = $(minimum(ratios_1inf):.3f), max = $(maximum(ratios_1inf):.3f)")
    println("Theoretical bounds: [1, $dim] = [1.000, $dim.000]")
end

test_norm_equivalence()

2.6 Product Metrics

When working with multiple metric spaces simultaneously, we need to define metrics on their Cartesian product.

Definition 2.16 (Product Metrics). Let (X₁, d₁) and (X₂, d₂) be metric spaces. On X₁ × X₂, we can define several product metrics: 1. d∞((x₁, x₂), (y₁, y₂)) = max(d₁(x₁, y₁), d₂(x₂, y₂)) 2. d₁((x₁, x₂), (y₁, y₂)) = d₁(x₁, y₁) + d₂(x₂, y₂) 3. d₂((x₁, x₂), (y₁, y₂)) = (d₁(x₁, y₁)² + d₂(x₂, y₂)²)^(1/2)

All three product metrics are equivalent and induce the same topology on X₁ × X₂.

Theorem 2.7 (Convergence in Product Spaces). A sequence (xₙ, yₙ) converges to (x, y) in X₁ × X₂ if and only if xₙ → x in X₁ and yₙ → y in X₂.

This theorem is fundamental for understanding convergence in multivariable settings and for proving results about continuous functions of several variables.

2.7 Applications to Modeling

2.7.1 Error Metrics for Model Calibration

In parameter estimation, we often minimize the distance between observed data and model predictions. The choice of metric significantly affects the resulting parameter estimates.

# Model calibration example: fitting an exponential model
using Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL
using Plots

# Generate synthetic data
true_params = [2.0, -0.5, 0.1]  # [A, λ, noise_std]
t_data = range(0, 5, length=20)
y_true = true_params[1] * exp.(true_params[2] * t_data)
y_data = y_true + true_params[3] * randn(length(t_data))

# Define model function
model(t, params) = params[1] * exp(params[2] * t)

# Different error metrics for calibration
function l2_error(params, data)
    t, y = data
    y_pred = model.(t, Ref(params))
    return sum((y - y_pred).^2)
end

function l1_error(params, data)
    t, y = data
    y_pred = model.(t, Ref(params))
    return sum(abs.(y - y_pred))
end

function linf_error(params, data)
    t, y = data
    y_pred = model.(t, Ref(params))
    return maximum(abs.(y - y_pred))
end

# Optimize using different metrics
data = (t_data, y_data)
initial_guess = [1.0, -1.0]

prob_l2 = OptimizationProblem((p, _) -> l2_error(p, data), initial_guess)
prob_l1 = OptimizationProblem((p, _) -> l1_error(p, data), initial_guess)
prob_linf = OptimizationProblem((p, _) -> linf_error(p, data), initial_guess)

sol_l2 = solve(prob_l2, NelderMead())
sol_l1 = solve(prob_l1, NelderMead())
sol_linf = solve(prob_linf, NelderMead())

println("True parameters: A = $(true_params[1]), λ = $(true_params[2])")
println("L² fit: A = $(sol_l2.u[1]:.3f), λ = $(sol_l2.u[2]:.3f)")
println("L¹ fit: A = $(sol_l1.u[1]:.3f), λ = $(sol_l1.u[2]:.3f)")
println("L∞ fit: A = $(sol_linf.u[1]:.3f), λ = $(sol_linf.u[2]:.3f)")

# Plot results
t_plot = range(0, 5, length=100)
plot(t_data, y_data, seriestype=:scatter, label="Data", markersize=4)
plot!(t_plot, model.(t_plot, Ref(sol_l2.u)), label="L² fit", linewidth=2)
plot!(t_plot, model.(t_plot, Ref(sol_l1.u)), label="L¹ fit", linewidth=2)
plot!(t_plot, model.(t_plot, Ref(sol_linf.u)), label="L∞ fit", linewidth=2)
xlabel!("Time")
ylabel!("Response")
title!("Model Fitting with Different Error Metrics")

2.7.2 Convergence of Numerical Methods

Metric spaces provide the natural framework for analyzing the convergence of iterative numerical methods.

Example 2.5 (Fixed Point Iteration). Consider solving f(x) = 0 by rewriting as x = g(x) and iterating xₙ₊₁ = g(xₙ). If g is a contraction mapping (satisfying d(g(x), g(y)) ≤ αd(x, y) for some α < 1), then the iteration converges to a unique fixed point.

# Fixed point iteration example
function fixed_point_iteration(g, x0, max_iter=100, tol=1e-8)
    x = x0
    history = [x]
    
    for i in 1:max_iter
        x_new = g(x)
        push!(history, x_new)
        
        if abs(x_new - x) < tol
            println("Converged after $i iterations")
            return x_new, history
        end
        
        x = x_new
    end
    
    println("Maximum iterations reached")
    return x, history
end

# Example: solve x³ - x - 1 = 0
# Rewrite as x = ∛(x + 1)
g(x) = cbrt(x + 1)

solution, history = fixed_point_iteration(g, 1.0)
println("Solution: x ≈ $solution")
println("Verification: x³ - x - 1 = $(solution^3 - solution - 1)")

# Plot convergence
p1 = plot(1:length(history), history, marker=:circle, 
          xlabel="Iteration", ylabel="x_n", title="Convergence of Fixed Point Iteration")

# Plot errors (distances from final value)
errors = abs.(history[1:end-1] .- solution)
p2 = plot(1:length(errors), errors, yscale=:log10, marker=:circle,
          xlabel="Iteration", ylabel="Error", title="Convergence Rate")

plot(p1, p2, layout=(1, 2), size=(800, 300))

2.7.3 Stability Analysis in Dynamical Systems

In modeling dynamical systems, we use metrics to quantify how perturbations to initial conditions affect the long-term behavior of solutions.

Example 2.6 (Sensitivity to Initial Conditions). Consider the logistic map xₙ₊₁ = rxₙ(1 - xₙ). For certain values of r, small changes in initial conditions lead to dramatically different trajectories.

# Demonstrate sensitive dependence on initial conditions
function logistic_map(x, r)
    return r * x * (1 - x)
end

function iterate_map(x0, r, n_steps)
    trajectory = zeros(n_steps + 1)
    trajectory[1] = x0
    
    for i in 1:n_steps
        trajectory[i+1] = logistic_map(trajectory[i], r)
    end
    
    return trajectory
end

# Parameters for chaotic regime
r = 3.9
n_steps = 100

# Two very close initial conditions
x0_1 = 0.5
x0_2 = 0.5 + 1e-10

traj1 = iterate_map(x0_1, r, n_steps)
traj2 = iterate_map(x0_2, r, n_steps)

# Calculate distance between trajectories over time
distances = abs.(traj1 - traj2)

p1 = plot(0:n_steps, [traj1 traj2], label=["x₀ = 0.5" "x₀ = 0.5 + 10⁻¹⁰"], 
          xlabel="Time", ylabel="x_n", title="Chaotic Trajectories")

p2 = plot(0:n_steps, distances, yscale=:log10, 
          xlabel="Time", ylabel="Distance", title="Exponential Divergence",
          linewidth=2)

plot(p1, p2, layout=(1, 2), size=(800, 300))

2.8 Exercises

Exercise 2.1 (Computational). Implement a Julia function that determines whether a given function d: X × X → ℝ satisfies the metric space axioms for a finite set X. Test your function on: a) The discrete metric on X = {1, 2, 3, 4} b) The function d(x, y) = |x - y|² on X = {1, 2,

3 Chapter 3: Compactness

Compactness is one of the most profound and useful concepts in real analysis, providing a bridge between the discrete and continuous worlds. In modeling contexts, compact sets often represent feasible parameter spaces or attainable states, and the properties of compactness guarantee the existence of optimal solutions and extrema that are essential for optimization theory and statistical inference.

3.1 3.1 Open Covers and Compactness

Definition 3.1 (Open Cover). Let (X, d) be a metric space and let A ⊆ X. An open cover of A is a collection {Uα}α∈I of open subsets of X such that A ⊆ ⋃α∈I Uα.

Definition 3.2 (Compactness). A subset K of a metric space (X, d) is called compact if every open cover of K has a finite subcover. That is, for any collection {Uα}α∈I of open sets with K ⊆ ⋃α∈I Uα, there exists a finite subset {α₁, α₂, …, αₙ} ⊆ I such that K ⊆ Uα₁ ∪ Uα₂ ∪ … ∪ Uαₙ.

This definition, while seemingly abstract, captures the geometric intuition that compact sets are “small” in a topological sense—they cannot be covered by infinitely many open sets without redundancy.

Theorem 3.3 (Basic Properties of Compact Sets). Let (X, d) be a metric space.

  1. Every compact subset of X is closed and bounded.
  2. If K is compact and F ⊆ K is closed, then F is compact.
  3. The union of finitely many compact sets is compact.
  4. If f: X → Y is continuous and K ⊆ X is compact, then f(K) is compact.

Proof. (a) Let K be compact. To show K is closed, we prove that X  K is open. For any x ∈ X  K, consider the open cover {B(k, d(x,k)/2) : k ∈ K} ∪ {B(x, d(x,K)/2)} where d(x,K) = inf{d(x,k) : k ∈ K} > 0. This cover has no finite subcover including the neighborhood of x, contradicting compactness unless we can extract a finite subcover not containing x’s neighborhood, which gives us an open neighborhood of x disjoint from K.

To show boundedness, the open cover {B(x₀, n) : n ∈ ℕ} for any fixed x₀ ∈ X must have a finite subcover, hence K is contained in some B(x₀, N).

(b), (c), and (d) follow from standard topological arguments using the definitions of closed sets, finite unions, and continuity. □

Example 3.1. Consider the parameter space Θ = {θ ∈ ℝⁿ : ||θ||₂ ≤ 1} for a regularized regression model. This unit ball is compact in ℝⁿ, guaranteeing that any continuous objective function L(θ) attains its minimum over Θ.

using LinearAlgebra, Plots

# Visualize compactness in R² - the unit disk
θ = range(0, 2π, length=1000)
unit_circle_x = cos.(θ)
unit_circle_y = sin.(θ)

plot(unit_circle_x, unit_circle_y, 
     seriestype=:shape, alpha=0.3, color=:blue,
     label="Compact unit disk", aspect_ratio=:equal)

# Generate random points inside the disk
n_points = 100
points = []
for i in 1:n_points
    r = sqrt(rand()) # sqrt for uniform distribution in disk
    angle = 2π * rand()
    push!(points, [r * cos(angle), r * sin(angle)])
end

scatter!([p[1] for p in points], [p[2] for p in points], 
         ms=2, alpha=0.6, color=:red, label="Feasible parameters")
title!("Compact Parameter Space in Regularized Regression")
xlabel!("θ₁")
ylabel!("θ₂")

3.2 3.2 Sequential Compactness

While the open cover definition of compactness is fundamental, sequential compactness provides a more intuitive and often more useful characterization in metric spaces.

Definition 3.4 (Sequential Compactness). A subset K of a metric space (X, d) is sequentially compact if every sequence in K has a subsequence that converges to a point in K.

Theorem 3.5 (Equivalence in Metric Spaces). In a metric space (X, d), a subset K is compact if and only if it is sequentially compact.

Proof. (⇒) Suppose K is compact but not sequentially compact. Then there exists a sequence (xₙ) in K with no convergent subsequence. This means for each x ∈ K, there exists εₓ > 0 such that B(x, εₓ) contains only finitely many terms of the sequence. The collection {B(x, εₓ) : x ∈ K} is an open cover of K, so has a finite subcover {B(xᵢ, εₓᵢ) : i = 1, …, m}. But then only finitely many terms of (xₙ) can lie in K, contradiction.

(⇐) Suppose K is sequentially compact but not compact. Then there exists an open cover {Uα}α∈I with no finite subcover. Construct a sequence as follows: since K ⊈ Uα₁ for some α₁, pick x₁ ∈ K  Uα₁. Since K ⊈ Uα₁ ∪ Uα₂ for some α₂, pick x₂ ∈ K  (Uα₁ ∪ Uα₂). Continue this process. The sequence (xₙ) has no convergent subsequence because any limit point would lie in some Uα, but infinitely many terms lie outside each finite union of the Uα. □

3.3 3.3 Compactness in Euclidean Space

In ℝⁿ with the standard Euclidean metric, compactness admits a particularly elegant characterization.

Theorem 3.6 (Heine-Borel Theorem). A subset K ⊆ ℝⁿ is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.

Proof. (⇒) We proved in Theorem 3.3(a) that compact sets are closed and bounded.

(⇐) Let K be closed and bounded in ℝⁿ. Since K is bounded, it lies in some closed hypercube Q = [−M, M]ⁿ. We first show Q is compact by showing it is sequentially compact. Any sequence in Q has a subsequence convergent in each coordinate (by the Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem in ℝ), and a diagonal argument shows this gives a convergent subsequence in Q. Since K is a closed subset of the compact set Q, K is compact by Theorem 3.3(b). □

Theorem 3.7 (Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem). Every bounded sequence in ℝⁿ has a convergent subsequence.

This fundamental result follows immediately from the Heine-Borel theorem and sequential compactness equivalence.

Example 3.2. In maximum likelihood estimation, consider the log-likelihood function ℓ(θ) for a statistical model with parameter θ ∈ Θ ⊆ ℝᵖ. If Θ is compact and ℓ is continuous, then the maximum likelihood estimator θ̂ = arg max_{θ∈Θ} ℓ(θ) exists.

using Distributions, Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL

# Example: MLE for normal distribution with bounded parameters
function negative_log_likelihood(θ, data)
    μ, σ = θ[1], θ[2]
    if σ ≤ 0
        return Inf  # Constraint violation
    end
    -sum(logpdf(Normal(μ, σ), x) for x in data)
end

# Generate synthetic data
Random.seed!(42)
true_μ, true_σ = 2.0, 1.5
data = rand(Normal(true_μ, true_σ), 100)

# Define compact parameter space: μ ∈ [-10, 10], σ ∈ [0.1, 10]
function constrained_nll(θ, data)
    μ, σ = θ[1], max(θ[2], 0.1)  # Ensure σ ≥ 0.1
    if abs(μ) > 10 || σ > 10
        return Inf
    end
    negative_log_likelihood([μ, σ], data)
end

# Optimization over compact parameter space
initial_guess = [0.0, 1.0]
prob = OptimizationProblem(
    (θ, p) -> constrained_nll(θ, data), 
    initial_guess
)

sol = solve(prob, BFGS())
mle_μ, mle_σ = sol.u

println("True parameters: μ = $true_μ, σ = $true_σ")
println("MLE estimates: μ = $(round(mle_μ, digits=3)), σ = $(round(mle_σ, digits=3))")
println("Optimization successful: $(sol.retcode)")

3.4 3.4 Total Boundedness and Compactness

In general metric spaces, the relationship between compactness and boundedness is more subtle. The key concept is total boundedness.

Definition 3.8 (Total Boundedness). A subset A of a metric space (X, d) is totally bounded if for every ε > 0, there exists a finite set of points {x₁, x₂, …, xₙ} ⊆ X such that A ⊆ ⋃ᵢ₌₁ⁿ B(xᵢ, ε).

Theorem 3.9 (Characterization of Compactness in Metric Spaces). Let (X, d) be a metric space and K ⊆ X. Then K is compact if and only if K is complete and totally bounded.

Proof. (⇒) If K is compact, then K is closed (hence complete as a subspace) and totally bounded (any ε-cover can be reduced to a finite subcover).

(⇐) Suppose K is complete and totally bounded. Let (xₙ) be any sequence in K. For each k ∈ ℕ, let {y₁^(k), …, yₙₖ^(k)} be a finite 1/k-net for K. Each xₙ lies within 1/k of some yᵢ^(k). By a diagonal argument, we can extract a subsequence that is Cauchy (since it becomes eventually concentrated near some points in each finite net). Since K is complete, this Cauchy subsequence converges in K. Therefore K is sequentially compact, hence compact. □

Example 3.3. Consider the space of continuous functions C([0,1]) with the supremum norm ||f||∞ = sup_{t∈[0,1]} |f(t)|. The unit ball {f : ||f||∞ ≤ 1} is bounded but not totally bounded, hence not compact. However, equicontinuous subsets can be totally bounded.

using Plots

# Demonstrate non-compactness of unit ball in C([0,1])
# by constructing a sequence with no convergent subsequence

function construct_bad_sequence(n_terms::Int)
    t_vals = range(0, 1, length=1000)
    functions = []
    
    for k in 1:n_terms
        # Construct function that's 1 on [k/(n_terms+1) - δ, k/(n_terms+1) + δ], 0 elsewhere
        # with smooth transitions
        center = k / (n_terms + 1)
        width = 1 / (4 * (n_terms + 1))
        
        f_vals = map(t -> begin
            if abs(t - center) ≤ width/2
                return 1.0
            elseif abs(t - center) ≤ width
                # Smooth transition
                return 0.5 * (1 + cos(π * (abs(t - center) - width/2) / (width/2)))
            else
                return 0.0
            end
        end, t_vals)
        
        push!(functions, (t_vals, f_vals))
    end
    
    return functions
end

# Visualize several functions from the non-convergent sequence
bad_seq = construct_bad_sequence(5)
plt = plot(title="Non-convergent Sequence in C([0,1])", 
          xlabel="t", ylabel="f(t)", legend=:topright)

for (i, (t_vals, f_vals)) in enumerate(bad_seq)
    plot!(plt, t_vals, f_vals, label="f_$i", linewidth=2)
end

display(plt)

# These functions are bounded (||f_k||∞ ≤ 1) but pairwise distance is ≥ 1
println("Pairwise supremum distances:")
for i in 1:length(bad_seq)-1
    for j in i+1:length(bad_seq)
        dist = maximum(abs.(bad_seq[i][2] .- bad_seq[j][2]))
        println("||f_$i - f_$j||∞ = $(round(dist, digits=3))")
    end
end

3.5 3.5 The Arzelà-Ascoli Theorem

The Arzelà-Ascoli theorem provides a crucial characterization of compact subsets in function spaces, with profound implications for analysis and modeling.

Definition 3.10 (Equicontinuity). A family ℱ of functions from a metric space (X, d₁) to a metric space (Y, d₂) is equicontinuous at x ∈ X if for every ε > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that for all f ∈ ℱ and all y ∈ X with d₁(x, y) < δ, we have d₂(f(x), f(y)) < ε. The family ℱ is equicontinuous if it is equicontinuous at every point of X.

Theorem 3.11 (Arzelà-Ascoli Theorem). Let X be a compact metric space and Y a complete metric space. A subset ℱ ⊆ C(X, Y) is compact in the supremum norm topology if and only if:

  1. ℱ is closed in C(X, Y),
  2. ℱ is pointwise bounded: for each x ∈ X, the set {f(x) : f ∈ ℱ} has compact closure in Y,
  3. ℱ is equicontinuous.

Proof sketch. The key insight is that equicontinuity provides the total boundedness needed for compactness. Given ε > 0, equicontinuity allows us to cover X with finitely many balls such that functions vary by at most ε/3 on each ball. Pointwise boundedness gives finite covers for function values at the centers of these balls. The finite combinations give an ε-net for ℱ. □

Example 3.4. In differential equation modeling, consider the family of solutions to ẋ = f(t, x) with ||f|| bounded. The solution family is equicontinuous by the Lipschitz property of the differential equation, making the Arzelà-Ascoli theorem applicable for existence proofs.

using DifferentialEquations, Plots

# Demonstrate equicontinuity for solutions of a bounded ODE
function vanderpol!(du, u, p, t)
    μ = p
    du[1] = u[2]
    du[2] = μ * (1 - u[1]^2) * u[2] - u[1]
end

# Solve for different initial conditions - forms an equicontinuous family
μ = 1.0
tspan = (0.0, 5.0)
initial_conditions = [
    [0.1, 0.1], [0.5, 0.2], [1.0, 0.0], 
    [1.5, -0.5], [2.0, 1.0], [-1.0, 0.5]
]

solutions = []
plt = plot(title="Equicontinuous Family of ODE Solutions", 
          xlabel="t", ylabel="x₁(t)")

for (i, u0) in enumerate(initial_conditions)
    prob = ODEProblem(vanderpol!, u0, tspan, μ)
    sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(), saveat=0.1)
    push!(solutions, sol)
    plot!(plt, sol.t, [u[1] for u in sol.u], 
          label="IC: ($(u0[1]), $(u0[2]))", linewidth=2)
end

display(plt)

# Verify equicontinuity numerically: max difference in derivatives
max_derivative_bound = 0.0
for sol in solutions
    for i in 1:length(sol.t)-1
        dt = sol.t[i+1] - sol.t[i]
        dx = norm(sol.u[i+1] - sol.u[i])
        derivative_bound = dx / dt
        max_derivative_bound = max(max_derivative_bound, derivative_bound)
    end
end

println("Maximum derivative bound: $(round(max_derivative_bound, digits=3))")
println("This provides the Lipschitz constant for equicontinuity")

3.6 3.6 Applications to Optimization

The fundamental importance of compactness in optimization cannot be overstated. The extreme value theorem is the cornerstone result.

Theorem 3.12 (Extreme Value Theorem). Let f: K → ℝ be a continuous function on a compact set K. Then f attains its maximum and minimum on K.

Proof. Since K is compact and f is continuous, f(K) is compact in ℝ. By the Heine-Borel theorem, f(K) is closed and bounded. Being bounded, f(K) has a supremum M = sup f(K). Since f(K) is closed and M is a limit point of f(K), we have M ∈ f(K). Therefore there exists x* ∈ K with f(x*) = M. Similarly for the minimum. □

Example 3.5. In portfolio optimization, consider maximizing expected return subject to a risk constraint. The feasible set {w ∈ ℝⁿ : ||w||₂ ≤ 1, ∑wᵢ = 1, wᵢ ≥ 0} is compact, guaranteeing an optimal portfolio exists.

using LinearAlgebra, Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL

# Portfolio optimization on compact feasible set
function portfolio_objective(w, (μ, Σ))
    # Negative expected return (we minimize)
    expected_return = dot(w, μ)
    risk_penalty = 0.5 * w' * Σ * w  # Risk penalty
    return -expected_return + 0.1 * risk_penalty
end

# Problem setup
n_assets = 4
Random.seed!(123)
μ = rand(n_assets) .* 0.1  # Expected returns
Σ = let A = randn(n_assets, n_assets)
    A' * A / n_assets + 0.01 * I  # Positive definite covariance
end

println("Expected returns: ", round.(μ, digits=4))
println("Risk matrix diagonal: ", round.(diag(Σ), digits=4))

# Constrained optimization over compact feasible set
function constrained_portfolio_obj(w, p)
    (μ, Σ) = p
    
    # Project onto feasible set (simplex with L2 bound)
    w_pos = max.(w, 0)  # Non-negativity
    if sum(w_pos) > 0
        w_pos = w_pos / sum(w_pos)  # Sum-to-one constraint
    else
        w_pos = ones(length(w)) / length(w)
    end
    
    # L2 bound constraint
    if norm(w_pos) > 1
        w_pos = w_pos / norm(w_pos)
    end
    
    return portfolio_objective(w_pos, p)
end

initial_w = ones(n_assets) / n_assets
prob = OptimizationProblem(constrained_portfolio_obj, initial_w, (μ, Σ))
sol = solve(prob, BFGS())

optimal_w = sol.u
# Project solution onto feasible set
optimal_w = max.(optimal_w, 0)
optimal_w = optimal_w / sum(optimal_w)
if norm(optimal_w) > 1
    optimal_w = optimal_w / norm(optimal_w)
end

println("\nOptimal portfolio weights: ", round.(optimal_w, digits=4))
println("Sum of weights: ", round(sum(optimal_w), digits=4))
println("L2 norm: ", round(norm(optimal_w), digits=4))
println("Expected return: ", round(dot(optimal_w, μ), digits=4))
println("Portfolio risk: ", round(sqrt(optimal_w' * Σ * optimal_w), digits=4))

3.7 3.7 Applications to Statistical Inference

In statistical inference, compactness of parameter spaces ensures the existence of maximum likelihood estimators and provides the foundation for asymptotic theory.

Theorem 3.13 (Existence of MLE on Compact Parameter Spaces). Let Θ be a compact subset of ℝᵈ and let ℓ(θ; x₁, …, xₙ) be a continuous log-likelihood function. Then the maximum likelihood estimator θ̂ₙ = arg max_{θ∈Θ} ℓ(θ; x₁, …, xₙ) exists.

This result is an immediate consequence of the extreme value theorem. Moreover, compactness provides crucial uniform bounds for asymptotic analysis.

Example 3.6. Consider robust regression with Huber loss, where we constrain regression coefficients to lie in a compact set to ensure regularization and existence of solutions.

using Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL, Plots

# Huber loss function
function huber_loss(r, δ=1.0)
    if abs(r) ≤ δ
        return 0.5 * r^2
    else
        return δ * (abs(r) - 0.5 * δ)
    end
end

# Generate data with outliers
Random.seed!(42)
n = 100
x_true = randn(n)
β_true = [2.0, -1.5]
y_clean = β_true[1] .+ β_true[2] * x_true
# Add outliers
outlier_indices = rand(1:n, 10)
y = copy(y_clean)
y[outlier_indices] .+= randn(10) * 5  # Large outliers

# Robust regression objective on compact parameter space
function robust_objective(β, (x, y))
    β₁, β₂ = β[1], β[2]
    
    # Constraint to compact set [-5, 5]²
    if abs(β₁) > 5 || abs(β₂) > 5
        return Inf
    end
    
    residuals = y .- (β₁ .+ β₂ * x)
    return sum(huber_loss.(residuals))
end

# Solve constrained robust regression
initial_β = [0.0, 0.0]
prob = OptimizationProblem(robust_objective, initial_β, (x_true, y))
sol = solve(prob, BFGS())

β_robust = sol.u
println("True parameters: β = $β_true")
println("Robust estimates: β = $(round.(β_robust, digits=3))")

# Compare with ordinary least squares
X_design = hcat(ones(n), x_true)
β_ols = (X_design' * X_design) \ (X_design' * y)
println("OLS estimates: β = $(round.(β_ols, digits=3))")

# Visualize results
scatter(x_true, y, alpha=0.6, label="Data", markersize=3)
scatter!(x_true[outlier_indices], y[outlier_indices], 
         color=:red, markersize=4, label="Outliers")

x_plot = range(minimum(x_true), maximum(x_true), length=100)
y_true = β_true[1] .+ β_true[2] * x_plot
y_robust = β_robust[1] .+ β_robust[2] * x_plot
y_ols_plot = β_ols[1] .+ β_ols[2] * x_plot

plot!(x_plot, y_true, linewidth=3, color=:green, label="True")
plot!(x_plot, y_robust, linewidth=3, color=:blue, label="Robust")
plot!(x_plot, y_ols_plot, linewidth=2, color=:orange, linestyle=:dash, label="OLS")
title!("Robust Regression on Compact Parameter Space")
xlabel!("x")
ylabel!("y")

3.8 3.8 Connections and Further Theory

Compactness provides the bridge between local and global properties in analysis. The concept extends naturally to infinite-dimensional spaces, where compact operators and compact embeddings play crucial roles in functional analysis and partial differential equations.

In measure theory, compactness is closely related to tightness of probability measures, providing the foundation for weak convergence theory essential in probability and statistics. The Prokhorov theorem characterizes relative compactness in the space of probability measures.

For modeling applications, compactness ensures the existence of optimal solutions in variational problems, provides uniform bounds for convergence theorems, and guarantees the stability of numerical algorithms through the finite covering property.

3.9 Exercises

  1. [Proof] Prove that the intersection of any family of compact subsets of a metric space is compact.

  2. [Computational] Implement a Julia function that numerically verifies the finite subcover property for a given compact set and open cover in ℝ².

function verify_finite_subcover(compact_set, open_cover, tolerance=1e-6)
    # Your implementation here
    # compact_set: function returning true if point is in set
    # open_cover: array of functions, each returning true if point is in that open set
    # Should return a minimal finite subcover
end

  1. [Theory] Show that in an infinite-dimensional normed space, the unit ball is never compact. Construct an explicit sequence in the unit ball of ℓ² with no convergent subsequence.

  2. [Application] Consider the optimization problem: minimize f(x, y) = x² + y² subject to g(x, y) = x³ + y³ - 1 = 0. Prove that the constraint set is compact and find the solution using Julia’s optimization tools.

  3. [Computational] Implement the Arzelà-Ascoli theorem verification by constructing a family of functions on [0,1], checking equicontinuity, and demonstrating compactness through subsequence convergence.

  4. [Theory] Prove that if K₁ and K₂ are compact sets in metric spaces X₁ and X₂ respectively, then K₁ × K₂ is compact in X₁ × X₂ with the product metric d((x₁,x₂), (y₁,y₂)) = d₁(x₁,y₁) + d₂(x₂,y₂).

  5. [Application] In Bayesian inference, the posterior distribution is proportional to prior × likelihood. If the prior is supported on a compact parameter space, prove that the posterior mode exists when the likelihood is continuous.

  6. [Computational] Implement a numerical method to find the Hausdorff distance between two compact sets in ℝ² and verify your implementation on the unit disk and unit square.

  7. [Theory] Prove the converse direction of the Arzelà-Ascoli theorem: if a subset of C([0,1]) is compact in the supremum norm, then it is closed, pointwise bounded, and equicontinuous.

  8. [Advanced] Consider the space of probability measures on [0,1] with the weak topology. Use Prokhorov’s theorem to characterize relatively compact subsets and implement a Julia function to verify tightness conditions for a given family of probability measures.

3.10 References

Rudin, W. (1976). Principles of Mathematical Analysis. McGraw-Hill.

Royden, H. L., & Fitzpatrick, P. M. (2010). Real Analysis. Prentice Hall.

Folland, G. B. (1999). Real Analysis: Modern Techniques and Their Applications. Wiley.

Munkres, J. R. (2000). Topology. Prentice Hall.

Billingsley, P. (1999). Convergence of Probability Measures. Wiley.

Conway, J. B. (1990). A Course in Functional Analysis. Springer.

4 Chapter 4: Connectedness and Continuity

The concept of connectedness captures our intuitive notion of a space being “in one piece,” while continuity formalizes the idea that small changes in input produce small changes in output. These seemingly elementary concepts have profound implications for modeling: the Intermediate Value Theorem guarantees the existence of equilibria, Lipschitz continuity ensures uniqueness of solutions to ordinary differential equations, and homeomorphisms preserve essential geometric structure under coordinate transformations.

This chapter develops the theory of connected spaces and continuous functions between metric spaces, with particular emphasis on properties that arise in mathematical modeling. We establish the fundamental theorems—the Intermediate Value Theorem, Extreme Value Theorem, and uniform continuity on compact sets—and explore their applications to existence theory for differential equations.

4.1 Connected Spaces and the Intermediate Value Theorem

4.1.1 Connectedness: Formal Definition and Basic Properties

Definition 4.1 (Connected Set). A subset E of a metric space (X, d) is connected if it cannot be written as the union of two nonempty, disjoint sets that are both open relative to E.

Equivalently, E is connected if whenever E = A ∪ B where A and B are nonempty, disjoint, and both open in the subspace topology on E, then either A = ∅ or B = ∅.

Definition 4.2 (Disconnected Set). A set E is disconnected if it is not connected, i.e., if there exist nonempty sets A, B such that: 1. A ∩ B = ∅ 2. A ∪ B = E
3. A and B are both open relative to E

The sets A and B in this case are called a separation of E.

Example 4.1. Consider E = [0,1) ∪ (2,3] ⊂ ℝ with the usual metric. Let A = [0,1) ∩ E = [0,1) and B = (2,3] ∩ E = (2,3]. Then A and B are nonempty and disjoint with A ∪ B = E. Moreover, A = E ∩ [0,1) is open relative to E, and B = E ∩ (2,3] is also open relative to E. Therefore E is disconnected.

Theorem 4.1 (Characterization of Connected Sets in ℝ). A subset E ⊂ ℝ is connected if and only if it is an interval.

Proof. First, suppose E is an interval but disconnected. Then there exist nonempty disjoint sets A, B, both open relative to E, with A ∪ B = E. Choose a ∈ A and b ∈ B with a < b (without loss of generality). Since E is an interval, [a,b] ⊂ E.

Define c = sup{x ∈ [a,b] : x ∈ A}. Since A is bounded above by b and nonempty, c exists and a ≤ c ≤ b. We claim c ∈ A.

If c ∉ A, then c ∈ B (since A ∪ B = E and c ∈ E). Since B is open relative to E, there exists r > 0 such that (c-r, c+r) ∩ E ⊂ B. But this contradicts the definition of c as the supremum of points in A ∩ [a,b], since there would be points arbitrarily close to c that belong to A.

Therefore c ∈ A. Since A is open relative to E, there exists r > 0 such that (c-r, c+r) ∩ E ⊂ A. This contradicts the fact that c is the supremum of A ∩ [a,b], since points greater than c would also belong to A.

Conversely, suppose E is not an interval. Then there exist a, b ∈ E and c ∉ E with a < c < b. Define A = E ∩ (-∞, c) and B = E ∩ (c, ∞). These sets are nonempty (containing a and b respectively), disjoint, open relative to E, and satisfy A ∪ B = E. Therefore E is disconnected. □

This characterization immediately yields the fundamental existence theorem for continuous functions on connected domains.

Theorem 4.2 (Intermediate Value Theorem). Let f: [a,b] → ℝ be continuous, and suppose f(a) < 0 < f(b). Then there exists c ∈ (a,b) such that f(c) = 0.

Proof. We prove the contrapositive. Suppose f(x) ≠ 0 for all x ∈ [a,b]. Define A = {x ∈ [a,b] : f(x) < 0} and B = {x ∈ [a,b] : f(x) > 0}. Since f is continuous, A = f⁻¹((-∞,0)) ∩ [a,b] and B = f⁻¹((0,∞)) ∩ [a,b] are both open relative to [a,b]. By hypothesis, A and B are nonempty (containing a and b respectively) and disjoint, with A ∪ B = [a,b]. This contradicts the connectedness of [a,b]. □

4.1.2 Computational Example: Finding Zeros Using Connectedness

The Intermediate Value Theorem not only guarantees existence of zeros but also underlies numerical root-finding algorithms like the bisection method.

using Plots

"""
Bisection method for finding zeros of continuous functions.
Based on the Intermediate Value Theorem.
"""
function bisection(f, a, b; tol=1e-10, max_iter=100)
    # Verify preconditions
    fa, fb = f(a), f(b)
    if fa * fb > 0
        error("f(a) and f(b) must have opposite signs")
    end
    
    # Track convergence
    iterations = Int[]
    intervals = Float64[]
    
    for i in 1:max_iter
        c = (a + b) / 2
        fc = f(c)
        
        push!(iterations, i)
        push!(intervals, b - a)
        
        if abs(fc) < tol || (b - a) / 2 < tol
            return c, iterations, intervals
        end
        
        # By IVT, zero lies in [a,c] or [c,b]
        if fa * fc < 0
            b, fb = c, fc
        else
            a, fa = c, fc
        end
    end
    
    error("Maximum iterations reached")
end

# Example: Find root of f(x) = x³ - 2x - 5
f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5

# Verify conditions: f(2) = -1 < 0, f(3) = 16 > 0
println("f(2) = $(f(2)), f(3) = $(f(3))")

root, iters, intervals = bisection(f, 2.0, 3.0)
println("Root found: x = $root")
println("f(root) = $(f(root))")
println("Converged in $(length(iters)) iterations")

# Visualize convergence
p1 = plot(f, 1.5, 3.5, label="f(x) = x³ - 2x - 5", lw=2)
hline!([0], color=:red, linestyle=:dash, label="y = 0")
vline!([root], color=:green, linestyle=:dash, label="Root ≈ $(round(root, digits=6))")
xlabel!("x")
ylabel!("f(x)")
title!("Intermediate Value Theorem Application")

p2 = plot(iters, log10.(intervals), marker=:circle, lw=2)
xlabel!("Iteration")
ylabel!("log₁₀(Interval Width)")
title!("Bisection Method Convergence")

plot(p1, p2, layout=(1,2), size=(800, 300))

4.1.3 Path-Connected Spaces

While connectedness captures the idea of a space being “in one piece,” path-connectedness provides a more intuitive geometric characterization.

Definition 4.3 (Path-Connected Set). A subset E of a metric space (X, d) is path-connected if for any two points x, y ∈ E, there exists a continuous function γ: [0,1] → E such that γ(0) = x and γ(1) = y. The function γ is called a path from x to y.

Theorem 4.3. Every path-connected set is connected.

Proof. Suppose E is path-connected but disconnected. Then there exist nonempty disjoint sets A, B, both open relative to E, with A ∪ B = E. Choose a ∈ A and b ∈ B. Since E is path-connected, there exists a continuous path γ: [0,1] → E with γ(0) = a and γ(1) = b.

Define A’ = γ⁻¹(A) and B’ = γ⁻¹(B). Since γ is continuous and A, B are open relative to E, both A’ and B’ are open relative to [0,1]. Moreover, A’ ∩ B’ = ∅, A’ ∪ B’ = [0,1], and both are nonempty (0 ∈ A’, 1 ∈ B’). This contradicts the connectedness of [0,1]. □

The converse is false in general: there exist connected sets that are not path-connected, such as the topologist’s sine curve {(x, sin(1/x)) : x ∈ (0,1]} ∪ {(0,0)}.

4.2 Continuous Functions Between Metric Spaces

4.2.1 Basic Definitions and Properties

Definition 4.4 (Continuity at a Point). Let (X, d_X) and (Y, d_Y) be metric spaces. A function f: X → Y is continuous at a point a ∈ X if for every ε > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that for all x ∈ X with d_X(x, a) < δ, we have d_Y(f(x), f(a)) < ε.

Definition 4.5 (Continuous Function). A function f: X → Y is continuous if it is continuous at every point of X.

Theorem 4.4 (Sequential Characterization of Continuity). A function f: X → Y is continuous at a ∈ X if and only if for every sequence (x_n) in X converging to a, the sequence (f(x_n)) converges to f(a).

Proof. (⟹) Suppose f is continuous at a and (x_n) → a. Let ε > 0. By continuity, there exists δ > 0 such that d_X(x, a) < δ implies d_Y(f(x), f(a)) < ε. Since x_n → a, there exists N such that n ≥ N implies d_X(x_n, a) < δ. Therefore, n ≥ N implies d_Y(f(x_n), f(a)) < ε, so f(x_n) → f(a).

(⟸) Suppose f is not continuous at a. Then there exists ε > 0 such that for every δ > 0, there exists x with d_X(x, a) < δ but d_Y(f(x), f(a)) ≥ ε. Taking δ = 1/n for each n ∈ ℕ, we obtain a sequence (x_n) with d_X(x_n, a) < 1/n but d_Y(f(x_n), f(a)) ≥ ε. Then x_n → a but f(x_n) does not converge to f(a), contradicting our assumption. □

4.2.2 Open and Closed Set Characterizations

Theorem 4.5 (Topological Characterization of Continuity). Let f: X → Y be a function between metric spaces. The following are equivalent: 1. f is continuous 2. For every open set U ⊂ Y, the preimage f⁻¹(U) is open in X 3. For every closed set F ⊂ Y, the preimage f⁻¹(F) is closed in X

Proof. (1 ⟹ 2) Suppose f is continuous and U ⊂ Y is open. Let x ∈ f⁻¹(U), so f(x) ∈ U. Since U is open, there exists ε > 0 such that B_Y(f(x), ε) ⊂ U. By continuity of f at x, there exists δ > 0 such that f(B_X(x, δ)) ⊂ B_Y(f(x), ε) ⊂ U. Therefore B_X(x, δ) ⊂ f⁻¹(U), proving that f⁻¹(U) is open.

(2 ⟹ 3) If f⁻¹(U) is open for every open U, then f⁻¹(F) = X  f⁻¹(Y  F) is closed for every closed F (since Y  F is open).

(3 ⟹ 1) Suppose f⁻¹(F) is closed for every closed F. Let x ∈ X and ε > 0. The set F = Y  B_Y(f(x), ε) is closed, so f⁻¹(F) is closed. Since x ∉ f⁻¹(F), there exists δ > 0 such that B_X(x, δ) ∩ f⁻¹(F) = ∅. This means f(B_X(x, δ)) ⊂ B_Y(f(x), ε), proving continuity at x. □

4.3 Uniform Continuity

4.3.1 Definition and Basic Properties

Continuity is a local property: the choice of δ may depend on both ε and the specific point. Uniform continuity strengthens this to a global condition.

Definition 4.6 (Uniform Continuity). A function f: X → Y between metric spaces is uniformly continuous if for every ε > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that for all x, x’ ∈ X with d_X(x, x’) < δ, we have d_Y(f(x), f(x’)) < ε.

Example 4.2. The function f(x) = x² on ℝ is continuous but not uniformly continuous. To see this, take ε = 1 and consider points x_n = n and x’_n = n + 1/(2n) for n ∈ ℕ. Then |x’_n - x_n| = 1/(2n) → 0, but |f(x’_n) - f(x_n)| = |n² + 1 + 1/(4n²) - n²| = 1 + 1/(4n²) ≥ 1. No fixed δ > 0 can work for all large n.

Theorem 4.6 (Uniform Continuity on Compact Sets). Every continuous function on a compact metric space is uniformly continuous.

Proof. Let f: X → Y be continuous where X is compact. Let ε > 0. For each x ∈ X, continuity gives δ_x > 0 such that f(B_X(x, δ_x)) ⊂ B_Y(f(x), ε/2). The collection {B_X(x, δ_x/2) : x ∈ X} is an open cover of X. By compactness, there exists a finite subcover {B_X(x_i, δ_{x_i}/2) : i = 1, …, n}.

Let δ = min{δ_{x_1}/2, …, δ_{x_n}/2} > 0. If d_X(x, x’) < δ, then x ∈ B_X(x_i, δ_{x_i}/2) for some i. Since d_X(x’, x_i) ≤ d_X(x’, x) + d_X(x, x_i) < δ + δ_{x_i}/2 ≤ δ_{x_i}, both f(x) and f(x’) lie in B_Y(f(x_i), ε/2). By the triangle inequality, d_Y(f(x), f(x’)) < ε. □

4.3.2 Computational Example: Testing Uniform Continuity

using Random, Statistics

"""
Numerical test for uniform continuity by sampling point pairs
and checking if the modulus of continuity remains bounded.
"""
function test_uniform_continuity(f, domain, ε=0.1; n_samples=10000, seed=42)
    Random.seed!(seed)
    
    # Generate random pairs of points
    if domain isa Tuple  # Interval [a,b]
        a, b = domain
        points1 = a .+ (b - a) .* rand(n_samples)
        points2 = a .+ (b - a) .* rand(n_samples)
    else  # Discrete set of points
        points1 = rand(domain, n_samples)
        points2 = rand(domain, n_samples)
    end
    
    # Compute distances and function differences
    x_diffs = abs.(points1 - points2)
    f_diffs = abs.(f.(points1) - f.(points2))
    
    # For each small x-difference, find maximum f-difference
    δ_values = range(0.001, 0.5, length=50)
    max_f_diffs = Float64[]
    
    for δ in δ_values
        # Find pairs with x-difference < δ
        close_pairs = x_diffs .< δ
        if sum(close_pairs) > 0
            max_f_diff = maximum(f_diffs[close_pairs])
        else
            max_f_diff = 0.0
        end
        push!(max_f_diffs, max_f_diff)
    end
    
    # Check if we can find δ such that max f-difference < ε
    suitable_δ = findfirst(max_f_diffs .< ε)
    
    return δ_values, max_f_diffs, suitable_δ
end

# Test uniform continuity of different functions
f1(x) = sin(x)  # Uniformly continuous on ℝ
f2(x) = x^2     # Not uniformly continuous on ℝ
f3(x) = x^2     # Uniformly continuous on [0,1]

# Test on unbounded domain
δ_vals, max_diffs, suitable = test_uniform_continuity(f2, (-10.0, 10.0))
println("f(x) = x² on [-10,10]:")
println("Can achieve ε = 0.1? $(suitable !== nothing)")

# Test on compact domain
δ_vals_compact, max_diffs_compact, suitable_compact = test_uniform_continuity(f3, (0.0, 1.0))
println("\nf(x) = x² on [0,1]:")
println("Can achieve ε = 0.1? $(suitable_compact !== nothing)")

# Visualize modulus of continuity
p1 = plot(δ_vals, max_diffs, label="x² on [-10,10]", lw=2, marker=:circle)
plot!(δ_vals_compact, max_diffs_compact, label="x² on [0,1]", lw=2, marker=:square)
hline!([0.1], color=:red, linestyle=:dash, label="ε = 0.1")
xlabel!("δ")
ylabel!("max |f(x) - f(y)| for |x-y| < δ")
title!("Modulus of Continuity")
legend!()

display(p1)

4.4 Lipschitz Continuity and Applications to ODEs
