If {f_n} is a sequence of measurable functions, then sup_n f_n, inf_n f_n, lim sup_n f_n, and lim inf_n f_n are measurable

These properties ensure that the class of measurable functions is robust under all the operations needed for analysis.

Example 8.3. The characteristic function χ_E of a set E is measurable if and only if E is measurable. For α < 0, {χ_E > α} = X. For 0 ≤ α < 1, {χ_E > α} = E. For α ≥ 1, {χ_E > α} = ∅.

6.5 8.5 Construction of the Lebesgue Integral

The Lebesgue integral is constructed in three stages: simple functions, non-negative functions, and general integrable functions.

6.5.1 Simple Functions

Definition 8.6 (Simple Function). A function φ: X → ℝ is simple if it takes only finitely many values. If φ = ∑{i=1}^n α_i χ{A_i} where the A_i are disjoint measurable sets with ⋃_i A_i = X, then

∫ φ dμ = ∑_{i=1}^n α_i μ(A_i)

This definition is well-posed: if μ(A_i) < ∞ whenever α_i ≠ 0, the integral is finite and independent of the representation.

6.5.2 Non-negative Functions

Definition 8.7 (Integral of Non-negative Functions). For a non-negative measurable function f: X → [0, ∞], define

∫ f dμ = sup{∫ φ dμ : φ simple, 0 ≤ φ ≤ f}

This supremum always exists (possibly infinite) and agrees with the simple function integral when f is simple.

The following fundamental theorem justifies this definition:

Theorem 8.6 (Monotone Convergence Theorem). Let {f_n} be a sequence of non-negative measurable functions with f_n ↗ f (i.e., f_n ≤ f_{n+1} for all n and f_n → f pointwise). Then

∫ f dμ = lim_{n→∞} ∫ f_n dμ

Proof. Since f_n ≤ f, we have ∫ f_n dμ ≤ ∫ f dμ, so lim ∫ f_n dμ ≤ ∫ f dμ. For the reverse inequality, let φ be simple with φ ≤ f. For 0 < c < 1, define E_n = {x : f_n(x) ≥ cφ(x)}. Then E_n ↗ X since f_n ↗ f ≥ φ. We have ∫ f_n dμ ≥ ∫{E_n} f_n dμ ≥ c ∫{E_n} φ dμ. Since μ is a measure and E_n ↗ X, we get lim ∫ f_n dμ ≥ c ∫ φ dμ. Taking c → 1 and the supremum over all such φ gives lim ∫ f_n dμ ≥ ∫ f dμ. □

6.5.3 General Integrable Functions

Definition 8.8 (Lebesgue Integrable Functions). A measurable function f: X → ℝ is Lebesgue integrable if ∫ |f| dμ < ∞. In this case, define

∫ f dμ = ∫ f^+ dμ - ∫ f^- dμ

where f^+ = max(f, 0) and f^- = max(-f, 0) are the positive and negative parts of f.

The space of integrable functions is denoted L¹(X, μ) or simply L¹ when the context is clear.

using QuadGK, Plots

# Demonstrate convergence issues with Riemann vs Lebesgue integrals
function modified_dirichlet(x)
    # Modified Dirichlet function: 1/n if x = p/q in lowest terms with q = n
    # 0 if x is irrational
    if x == 0
        return 1.0
    end
    
    # For computational purposes, approximate with rational approximations
    # In practice, this function equals 0 almost everywhere
    tolerance = 1e-10
    for n in 1:100
        for p in 1:n
            if abs(x - p/n) < tolerance && gcd(p, n) == 1
                return 1.0/n
            end
        end
    end
    return 0.0
end

# Lebesgue integrable function that's not Riemann integrable
function integrable_but_not_riemann(x)
    # This function equals 0 except on a countable set
    return modified_dirichlet(x)
end

# Demonstrate pointwise convergence that preserves Lebesgue integral
function tent_function(x, n)
    # Sequence of functions: n*max(0, 1 - n*|x|)
    # Converges pointwise to 0 but integral remains 1
    if abs(x) ≤ 1/n
        return n * (1 - n*abs(x))
    else
        return 0.0
    end
end

# Visualize the tent function sequence
n_values = [1, 2, 5, 10, 20]
x_vals = -0.5:0.001:0.5
plt = plot(title="Tent Functions: Pointwise → 0, but ∫f_n = 1", 
           xlabel="x", ylabel="f_n(x)")

for n in n_values
    y_vals = [tent_function(x, n) for x in x_vals]
    plot!(plt, x_vals, y_vals, label="n=$n", linewidth=2)
end

display(plt)

# Compute integrals using quadrature
println("Integrals of tent functions:")
for n in [1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50]
    integral_val, _ = quadgk(x -> tent_function(x, n), -1, 1)
    println("n = $n: ∫f_n dx = $(round(integral_val, digits=4))")
end

6.6 8.6 The Fundamental Convergence Theorems

The power of Lebesgue integration lies in its superior convergence theorems, which resolve the pathologies encountered in Riemann integration.

Theorem 8.7 (Fatou’s Lemma). If {f_n} is a sequence of non-negative measurable functions, then

∫ (lim inf_{n→∞} f_n) dμ ≤ lim inf_{n→∞} ∫ f_n dμ

Proof. Let g_k = inf_{n≥k} f_n. Then g_k ↗ lim inf f_n and g_k ≤ f_n for n ≥ k. By monotone convergence, ∫ g_k dμ ↗ ∫ (lim inf f_n) dμ. Since ∫ g_k dμ ≤ inf_{n≥k} ∫ f_n dμ, we get ∫ (lim inf f_n) dμ ≤ lim_{k→∞} inf_{n≥k} ∫ f_n dμ = lim inf ∫ f_n dμ. □

Theorem 8.8 (Dominated Convergence Theorem). Let {f_n} be a sequence of measurable functions converging pointwise to f, and suppose there exists an integrable function g such that |f_n(x)| ≤ g(x) for all n and x. Then f is integrable and

lim_{n→∞} ∫ f_n dμ = ∫ f dμ

Proof. Since |f| ≤ g almost everywhere, f is integrable. Apply Fatou’s lemma to g + f_n ≥ 0 and g - f_n ≥ 0:

∫ g dμ + ∫ f dμ = ∫ (g + f) dμ ≤ lim inf ∫ (g + f_n) dμ = ∫ g dμ + lim inf ∫ f_n dμ

∫ g dμ - ∫ f dμ = ∫ (g - f) dμ ≤ lim inf ∫ (g - f_n) dμ = ∫ g dμ - lim sup ∫ f_n dμ

These give ∫ f dμ ≤ lim inf ∫ f_n dμ and ∫ f dμ ≥ lim sup ∫ f_n dμ, so the limit exists and equals ∫ f dμ. □

These theorems demonstrate why Lebesgue integration is essential for modern analysis: they provide the convergence guarantees needed for rigorous limit processes.

Example 8.4 (Failure of Dominated Convergence for Riemann Integrals). Consider f_n(x) = n²x e^{-nx} on [0,1]. These are continuous, hence Riemann integrable. We have ∫₀¹ f_n(x) dx = n - 1 + e^{-n} → ∞, yet f_n(x) → 0 pointwise. The issue is that no single integrable function dominates the entire sequence—the functions develop increasingly sharp spikes near x = 0.

6.7 8.7 Comparison with Riemann Integration

Understanding the relationship between Riemann and Lebesgue integration clarifies when each theory applies.

Theorem 8.9 (Riemann-Lebesgue Theorem). Let f be a bounded function on [a,b]. Then f is Riemann integrable if and only if the set of discontinuities of f has Lebesgue measure zero. In this case, the Riemann and Lebesgue integrals agree.

The proof relies on characterizing Riemann integrability via oscillation: f is Riemann integrable if and only if for every ε > 0, the set where f oscillates by more than ε has arbitrarily small total length.

This theorem immediately shows that many functions are Lebesgue integrable but not Riemann integrable:

Example 8.5. The function f(x) = χ_ℚ (indicator of rationals) on [0,1] is not Riemann integrable since it’s discontinuous everywhere, but it is Lebesgue integrable with ∫ f dμ = 0 since μ(ℚ ∩ [0,1]) = 0.

The advantages of Lebesgue integration include:

  1. Larger class of integrable functions: Every bounded function with countably many discontinuities is Lebesgue integrable
  2. Superior convergence theorems: Dominated convergence, monotone convergence, and Fatou’s lemma
  3. Measure-theoretic foundation: Natural integration theory for probability spaces
  4. Completeness: L¹ spaces are complete metric spaces, unlike Riemann integrable functions
using Random, Statistics, Distributions

# Demonstrate superior convergence properties
Random.seed!(123)

# Example: Monte Carlo integration using Lebesgue theory
function monte_carlo_integral(f, a, b, n_samples)
    # Estimate ∫_a^b f(x) dx using uniform random sampling
    samples = rand(Uniform(a, b), n_samples)
    f_values = [f(x) for x in samples]
    return (b - a) * mean(f_values)
end

# Function with jump discontinuities (Lebesgue integrable, not Riemann)
function step_function(x)
    if x < 0.25
        return 1.0
    elseif x < 0.5
        return 2.0
    elseif x < 0.75
        return 1.5
    else
        return 0.5
    end
end

# Theoretical integral of step function
theoretical_integral = 0.25 * 1.0 + 0.25 * 2.0 + 0.25 * 1.5 + 0.25 * 0.5
println("Theoretical integral: $theoretical_integral")

# Monte Carlo estimates with increasing sample sizes
sample_sizes = [100, 1000, 10000, 100000]
estimates = []

for n in sample_sizes
    estimate = monte_carlo_integral(step_function, 0, 1, n)
    push!(estimates, estimate)
    error = abs(estimate - theoretical_integral)
    println("n = $n: estimate = $(round(estimate, digits=4)), error = $(round(error, digits=4))")
end

# Demonstrate convergence rate
println("\nConvergence analysis:")
for i in 2:length(estimates)
    rate = log(abs(estimates[i] - theoretical_integral) / abs(estimates[i-1] - theoretical_integral)) / 
           log(sample_sizes[i-1] / sample_sizes[i])
    println("Rate from $(sample_sizes[i-1]) to $(sample_sizes[i]): $(round(rate, digits=2))")
end

6.8 8.8 Preview of Fubini’s Theorem

One of the most powerful applications of Lebesgue integration is Fubini’s theorem, which provides conditions under which double integrals can be computed as iterated integrals. While the full development requires product measures (covered in advanced measure theory), we can state the result for functions on rectangles.

Theorem 8.10 (Fubini’s Theorem, Simplified Version). Let f: [a,b] × [c,d] → ℝ be measurable. If any of the following equivalent conditions holds: 1. f ≥ 0 almost everywhere 2. ∫∫_{[a,b]×[c,d]} |f(x,y)| dx dy < ∞

Then ∫∫_{[a,b]×[c,d]} f(x,y) dx dy = ∫_c^d (∫_a^b f(x,y) dx) dy = ∫_a^b (∫_c^d f(x,y) dy) dx

The power of this theorem is that it applies to many functions for which the classical Fubini theorem fails due to integrability issues.

Example 8.6. Consider f(x,y) = xy/(x² + y²)^{3/2} on [0,1] × [0,1] ∖ {(0,0)}. This function is not absolutely integrable in the Riemann sense due to the singularity at the origin, but the improper Lebesgue integral exists, and Fubini’s theorem applies after proper regularization.

using SpecialFunctions

# Numerical demonstration of Fubini's theorem
function fubini_example_integrand(x, y)
    # f(x,y) = exp(-(x^2 + y^2)) * sin(xy)
    # This is absolutely integrable over R^2
    return exp(-(x^2 + y^2)) * sin(x * y)
end

# Compute double integral by iterated integration
function integrate_xy_order(x_range, y_range, f, n_points=100)
    x_vals = range(x_range[1], x_range[2], length=n_points)
    y_vals = range(y_range[1], y_range[2], length=n_points)
    dx = (x_range[2] - x_range[1]) / (n_points - 1)
    dy = (y_range[2] - y_range[1]) / (n_points - 1)
    
    integral = 0.0
    for x in x_vals
        inner_integral = 0.0
        for y in y_vals
            inner_integral += f(x, y) * dy
        end
        integral += inner_integral * dx
    end
    return integral
end

function integrate_yx_order(x_range, y_range, f, n_points=100)
    x_vals = range(x_range[1], x_range[2], length=n_points)
    y_vals = range(y_range[1], y_range[2], length=n_points)
    dx = (x_range[2] - x_range[1]) / (n_points - 1)
    dy = (y_range[2] - y_range[1]) / (n_points - 1)
    
    integral = 0.0
    for y in y_vals
        inner_integral = 0.0
        for x in x_vals
            inner_integral += f(x, y) * dx
        end
        integral += inner_integral * dy
    end
    return integral
end

# Test Fubini's theorem numerically
x_range = (-2.0, 2.0)
y_range = (-2.0, 2.0)

integral_xy = integrate_xy_order(x_range, y_range, fubini_example_integrand, 200)
integral_yx = integrate_yx_order(x_range, y_range, fubini_example_integrand, 200)

println("∫∫ f(x,y) dx dy = $(round(integral_xy, digits=6))")
println("∫∫ f(x,y) dy dx = $(round(integral_yx, digits=6))")
println("Difference: $(round(abs(integral_xy - integral_yx), digits=8))")

# For comparison, the exact value can be computed using the fact that
# ∫∫ exp(-(x^2+y^2)) sin(xy) dx dy = 0 by symmetry
println("Theoretical


\newpage

# Chapter 9: Lp Spaces: A First Look at Function Spaces

The spaces of functions that arise in modeling are seldom arbitrary collections. Rather, they possess geometric structure that mirrors finite-dimensional vector spaces while accommodating the infinite-dimensional nature of function spaces. The Lp spaces, introduced in this chapter, exemplify this principle. They provide the foundation for modern analysis of partial differential equations, optimization theory, and probability theory.

In modeling contexts, we rarely work with individual functions but rather with classes of functions sharing common properties. The question of convergence—when does a sequence of approximate solutions converge to the true solution?—requires a notion of distance between functions. The Lp spaces provide precisely this structure, generalizing the familiar Euclidean geometry of finite dimensions to infinite-dimensional function spaces.

## 9.1 Definition and Basic Properties of Lp Spaces

Consider a measure space (X, Σ, μ). For a measurable function f: X → ℝ (or ℂ), we define its Lp norm for 1 ≤ p < ∞ as:

||f||p = (∫X |f(x)|p dμ(x))^(1/p)

For p = ∞, we define:

||f||∞ = ess sup{|f(x)| : x ∈ X}

where the essential supremum is the infimum of all M such that |f(x)| ≤ M almost everywhere.

> **Definition 9.1 (Lp Space).** For 1 ≤ p ≤ ∞, the Lp space with respect to the measure μ is:
>
> Lp(X, μ) = {f: X → ℝ measurable : ||f||p < ∞}
>
> When the measure space is clear from context, we write simply Lp(X) or Lp.

The most common cases are X = ℝ with Lebesgue measure, X = [a,b] with Lebesgue measure, or X discrete with counting measure. The space L2 holds special significance as it possesses inner product structure, making it a Hilbert space.

A crucial observation is that ||f||p = 0 if and only if f = 0 almost everywhere. This means Lp is not quite a normed space in the strict sense, since distinct functions can have zero distance. We resolve this by considering equivalence classes of functions that agree almost everywhere.

> **Definition 9.2 (Lp as a Normed Space).** The space Lp(X, μ) is the quotient space of measurable functions with finite Lp norm, where we identify functions that agree μ-almost everywhere. The Lp norm on this quotient space satisfies all axioms of a norm.

The geometric intuition is clear: just as ℝn with the p-norm ||x||p = (Σ|xi|p)^(1/p) forms a normed space, the function space Lp inherits analogous structure. The parameter p controls the "shape" of the unit ball, just as in finite dimensions.

### Example 9.1: Computing Lp Norms

Consider the function f(x) = x^(-1/3) on [0,1]. Let us compute ||f||p for various values of p.

For 1 ≤ p < 3:
||f||p^p = ∫₀¹ x^(-p/3) dx = [x^(1-p/3)/(1-p/3)]₀¹ = 1/(1-p/3)

Therefore ||f||p = (1/(1-p/3))^(1/p). This is finite for p < 3, so f ∈ Lp([0,1]) for 1 ≤ p < 3.

For p ≥ 3, the integral diverges, so f ∉ Lp([0,1]).

For p = ∞, since f is unbounded near 0, we have ||f||∞ = ∞, so f ∉ L∞([0,1]).

This example illustrates that the inclusion relationships between Lp spaces depend on the measure of the domain. On finite measure spaces, we have Lq ⊆ Lp for p ≤ q, while on infinite measure spaces, the inclusions can reverse.

```julia
using Plots, QuadGK

# Visualize the function f(x) = x^(-1/3) and compute Lp norms numerically
function compute_lp_norm(p, f, a, b; tol=1e-10)
    if p == Inf
        # For L∞ norm, we approximate by taking maximum over dense grid
        x_vals = range(a + tol, b, length=10000)
        return maximum(abs.(f.(x_vals)))
    else
        integrand(x) = abs(f(x))^p
        integral, _ = quadgk(integrand, a + tol, b)
        return integral^(1/p)
    end
end

f(x) = x^(-1/3)
p_values = [1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 2.9, 2.99]
norms = [compute_lp_norm(p, f, 0, 1) for p in p_values]

println("Lp norms of f(x) = x^(-1/3) on [0,1]:")
for (p, norm) in zip(p_values, norms)
    println("p = $p: ||f||_p = $(round(norm, digits=4))")
end

# Visualize the function
x = range(0.01, 1, length=1000)
plot(x, f.(x), xlabel="x", ylabel="f(x)", title="f(x) = x^(-1/3)", 
     linewidth=2, xlims=(0, 1))

6.9 9.2 Hölder’s and Minkowski’s Inequalities

The fundamental inequalities that make Lp spaces into normed spaces are Hölder’s inequality and its consequence, Minkowski’s inequality. These generalizations of the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality are essential for proving completeness and establishing duality relationships.

Theorem 9.3 (Hölder’s Inequality). Let 1 < p < ∞ and let q be the conjugate exponent defined by 1/p + 1/q = 1. If f ∈ Lp(X, μ) and g ∈ Lq(X, μ), then fg ∈ L1(X, μ) and

∫X |f(x)g(x)| dμ(x) ≤ ||f||p ||g||q

Proof. The key insight is Young’s inequality: for a, b ≥ 0, ab ≤ (a^p)/p + (b^q)/q

with equality if and only if a^p = b^q.

If either ||f||p = 0 or ||g||q = 0, the inequality holds trivially. Otherwise, applying Young’s inequality with a = |f(x)|/||f||p and b = |g(x)|/||g||q:

|f(x)g(x)|/(||f||p||g||q) ≤ |f(x)|p/(p||f||pp) + |g(x)|q/(q||g||qq)

Integrating both sides: ∫X |f(x)g(x)|dμ(x)/(||f||p||g||q) ≤ 1/p + 1/q = 1

Therefore ∫X |f(x)g(x)|dμ(x) ≤ ||f||p||g||q. □

Theorem 9.4 (Minkowski’s Inequality). For 1 ≤ p ≤ ∞, if f, g ∈ Lp(X, μ), then f + g ∈ Lp(X, μ) and

||f + g||p ≤ ||f||p + ||g||p

Proof. The cases p = 1 and p = ∞ follow from the triangle inequality for real numbers. For 1 < p < ∞:

||f + g||p^p = ∫X |f + g|^p dμ ≤ ∫X |f + g|^(p-1)(|f| + |g|) dμ = ∫X |f + g|^(p-1)|f| dμ + ∫X |f + g|^(p-1)|g| dμ

Since (p-1)q = p where q is the conjugate of p, we have |f + g|^(p-1) ∈ Lq. Applying Hölder’s inequality to each term:

∫X |f + g|^(p-1)|f| dμ ≤ (∫X |f + g|^p dμ)^(1/q)||f||p ∫X |f + g|^(p-1)|g| dμ ≤ (∫X |f + g|^p dμ)^(1/q)||g||p

Therefore: ||f + g||p^p ≤ ||f + g||p^(p/q)(||f||p + ||g||p)

Since p/q = p - 1, dividing by ||f + g||p^(p-1) gives ||f + g||p ≤ ||f||p + ||g||p. □

These inequalities establish that Lp is indeed a normed vector space. The geometric interpretation is that Hölder’s inequality generalizes the fact that the dot product of two vectors is bounded by the product of their norms, while Minkowski’s inequality ensures the triangle inequality holds.

6.9.1 Example 9.2: Hölder’s Inequality in Action

Consider f(x) = x^(1/4) and g(x) = x^(-1/8) on [0,1]. Let p = 4 and q = 4/3 (so 1/p + 1/q = 1).

We compute: ||f||₄⁴ = ∫₀¹ x dx = 1/2, so ||f||₄ = (1/2)^(1/4) ||g||{4/3}^{4/3} = ∫₀¹ x^(-1/6) dx = [x^(5/6)/(5/6)]₀¹ = 6/5, so ||g||{4/3} = (6/5)^(3/4)

The product fg(x) = x^(1/4-1/8) = x^(1/8), so: ∫₀¹ |f(x)g(x)| dx = ∫₀¹ x^(1/8) dx = [x^(9/8)/(9/8)]₀¹ = 8/9

Hölder’s inequality predicts: 8/9 ≤ (1/2)^(1/4) · (6/5)^(3/4) ≈ 0.94, which indeed holds.

# Verify Hölder's inequality numerically
using QuadGK

function verify_holder(f, g, p, a, b)
    q = p / (p - 1)  # Conjugate exponent
    
    # Compute ||f||_p
    f_norm_p = (quadgk(x -> abs(f(x))^p, a, b)[1])^(1/p)
    
    # Compute ||g||_q  
    g_norm_q = (quadgk(x -> abs(g(x))^q, a, b)[1])^(1/q)
    
    # Compute ∫|fg|
    fg_integral = quadgk(x -> abs(f(x) * g(x)), a, b)[1]
    
    println("||f||_$p = $(round(f_norm_p, digits=6))")
    println("||g||_$q = $(round(g_norm_q, digits=6))")
    println("∫|fg| = $(round(fg_integral, digits=6))")
    println("||f||_p * ||g||_q = $(round(f_norm_p * g_norm_q, digits=6))")
    println("Hölder satisfied: $(fg_integral ≤ f_norm_p * g_norm_q)")
    
    return fg_integral, f_norm_p * g_norm_q
end

f(x) = x^(1/4)
g(x) = x^(-1/8)
verify_holder(f, g, 4.0, 0.001, 1.0)  # Avoid singularity at 0

6.10 9.3 Completeness of Lp Spaces: The Riesz-Fischer Theorem

The completeness of Lp spaces is perhaps their most important property for analysis. It ensures that Cauchy sequences of functions converge within the space, enabling the application of powerful theorems from functional analysis.

Theorem 9.5 (Riesz-Fischer Theorem). For 1 ≤ p ≤ ∞, the space Lp(X, μ) is complete. That is, every Cauchy sequence in Lp converges to a limit in Lp.

Proof. Let {fn} be a Cauchy sequence in Lp. We construct the limit function through a subsequence argument.

Since {fn} is Cauchy, we can extract a subsequence {fnₖ} such that ||fnₖ₊₁ - fnₖ||p < 2^(-k) for all k.

Define gₖ = |fn₁| + Σⱼ₌₁^(k-1) |fnⱼ₊₁ - fnⱼ|. By Minkowski’s inequality:

||gₖ||p ≤ ||fn₁||p + Σⱼ₌₁^(k-1) ||fnⱼ₊₁ - fnⱼ||p ≤ ||fn₁||p + Σⱼ₌₁^(k-1) 2^(-j) < ||fn₁||p + 1

The sequence {gₖ} is increasing and bounded in Lp. By monotone convergence, g = lim gₖ exists and ||g||p ≤ ||fn₁||p + 1.

Since gₖ(x) → g(x) pointwise and gₖ(x) ≥ |fnₖ(x)|, the subsequence {fnₖ(x)} converges absolutely for almost every x. Define f(x) as this pointwise limit where it exists, and f(x) = 0 otherwise.

The dominated convergence theorem shows that fnₖ → f in Lp. Since the original sequence {fn} was Cauchy and has a convergent subsequence, the entire sequence converges to f. □

This theorem is fundamental because it establishes Lp spaces as Banach spaces (complete normed vector spaces). The completeness property enables the use of fixed point theorems and ensures that variational problems have solutions within the space.

6.10.1 Example 9.3: Convergence in Lp vs Pointwise Convergence

Consider the sequence of functions on [0,1]: fn(x) = n χ0,1/n

where χA denotes the characteristic function of set A.

For any 1 ≤ p < ∞: ||fn||p^p = ∫₀^(1/n) n^p dx = n^p · (1/n) = n^(p-1) → ∞

So {fn} does not converge in Lp for p ≥ 1. However, fn(x) → 0 pointwise for every x > 0, and fn(0) = n → ∞.

This example illustrates that pointwise convergence and Lp convergence are distinct concepts. The sequence concentrates mass at a single point while spreading thinner elsewhere.

using Plots

# Visualize the sequence f_n(x) = n * χ[0,1/n](x)
function f_n(x, n)
    return (0 ≤ x ≤ 1/n) ? n : 0.0
end

# Plot several terms of the sequence
x = range(0, 1, length=1000)
plot(layout=(2,2), size=(800, 600))

for (i, n) in enumerate([1, 5, 20, 100])
    y = [f_n(xi, n) for xi in x]
    plot!(x, y, subplot=i, title="n = $n", xlabel="x", ylabel="f_n(x)", 
          xlims=(0, 0.5), ylims=(0, max(n+1, 10)), linewidth=2)
end

# Compute L^p norms for different n values
ns = 1:20
l1_norms = [1.0 for n in ns]  # ||f_n||_1 = 1 for all n
l2_norms = [sqrt(n) for n in ns]  # ||f_n||_2 = √n
linf_norms = float.(ns)  # ||f_n||_∞ = n

plot(ns, [l1_norms l2_norms linf_norms], 
     labels=["L¹" "L²" "L∞"], 
     xlabel="n", ylabel="||f_n||_p", 
     title="Lp Norms of f_n", linewidth=2, legend=:topleft)

6.11 9.4 L2 as a Hilbert Space

The space L2 possesses additional structure beyond that of a general Banach space: it admits an inner product that induces the L2 norm. This makes L2 a Hilbert space, the infinite-dimensional analogue of Euclidean space.

Definition 9.6 (L2 Inner Product). For f, g ∈ L2(X, μ), define the inner product:

⟨f, g⟩ = ∫X f(x)g(x) dμ(x)

For complex-valued functions, ⟨f, g⟩ = ∫X f(x)g(x)* dμ(x).

The inner product satisfies: 1. Linearity in the first argument: ⟨af + bg, h⟩ = a⟨f, h⟩ + b⟨g, h⟩ 2. Conjugate symmetry: ⟨f, g⟩ = ⟨g, f⟩* 3. Positive definiteness: ⟨f, f⟩ ≥ 0 with equality if and only if f = 0 a.e.

The norm induced by this inner product is precisely the L2 norm: ||f||₂ = √⟨f, f⟩.

6.11.1 Orthogonality and Projections

Two functions f, g ∈ L2 are orthogonal if ⟨f, g⟩ = 0. A set {eₐ} ⊆ L2 is orthonormal if ⟨eₐ, eβ⟩ = δₐβ (Kronecker delta). The Gram-Schmidt process constructs orthonormal sets from linearly independent sets.

Theorem 9.7 (Projection Theorem). Let M be a closed subspace of the Hilbert space H. For any f ∈ H, there exists a unique element PM f ∈ M such that ||f - PM f|| = inf{||f - g|| : g ∈ M}. Moreover, f - PM f ⊥ M.

This theorem enables us to decompose any function into components parallel and perpendicular to a given subspace, generalizing the familiar notion of vector projection.

6.11.2 Fourier Series as Orthogonal Projections

The classical Fourier series provides the most important example of orthogonal projection in L2. On the interval [-π, π], the functions:

{1/√(2π), cos(nx)/√π, sin(nx)/√π : n = 1, 2, 3, …}

form an orthonormal system in L2[-π, π].

Theorem 9.8 (Fourier Series Convergence in L2). For f ∈ L2[-π, π], the Fourier series

Sₙ(f)(x) = a₀/2 + Σₖ₌₁ⁿ (aₖ cos(kx) + bₖ sin(kx))

where aₖ = (1/π)∫₋π^π f(t)cos(kt)dt and bₖ = (1/π)∫₋π^π f(t)sin(kt)dt, satisfies ||f - Sₙ(f)||₂ → 0 as n → ∞.

This theorem shows that any L2 function can be approximated arbitrarily well in the L2 norm by trigonometric polynomials. The Fourier coefficients arise naturally as inner products with the orthonormal basis functions.

6.11.3 Example 9.4: Fourier Series of a Square Wave

Consider the square wave f(x) = 1 for 0 < x < π and f(x) = -1 for -π < x < 0, extended periodically.

The Fourier coefficients are: - a₀ = 0 (the function has zero mean) - aₖ = 0 for k ≥ 1 (the function is odd) - bₖ = (4/(kπ)) for k odd, bₖ = 0 for k even

The Fourier series is: f(x) ~ (4/π)[sin(x) + sin(3x)/3 + sin(5x)/5 + …]

using Plots, FFTW

# Define the square wave and compute its Fourier series approximation
function square_wave(x)
    x_mod = mod(x + π, 2π) - π
    return x_mod > 0 ? 1.0 : -1.0
end

function fourier_approximation(x, n_terms)
    result = 0.0
    for k in 1:2:n_terms  # Only odd terms
        result += (4 / (k * π)) * sin(k * x)
    end
    return result
end

# Visualize the approximation
x = range(-2π, 2π, length=1000)
f_exact = square_wave.(x)

plot(x, f_exact, label="Square wave", linewidth=2, linestyle=:solid)
for n in [1, 3, 9, 19]
    f_approx = [fourier_approximation(xi, n) for xi in x]
    plot!(x, f_approx, label="N = $n terms", linewidth=2)
end

xlabel!("x")
ylabel!("f(x)")
title!("Fourier Series Approximation of Square Wave")
xlims!(-2π, 2π)
ylims!(-1.5, 1.5)

# Compute L² convergence
function l2_error(n_terms)
    x_fine = range(-π, π, length=10000)
    f_exact = square_wave.(x_fine)
    f_approx = [fourier_approximation(xi, n_terms) for xi in x_fine]
    dx = x_fine[2] - x_fine[1]
    return sqrt(sum((f_exact - f_approx).^2) * dx)
end

ns = 1:2:20
errors = [l2_error(n) for n in ns]
plot(ns, errors, xlabel="Number of terms", ylabel="L² error", 
     title="L² Convergence of Fourier Series", 
     linewidth=2, marker=:circle, yscale=:log10)

6.12 9.5 Dense Subsets: Continuous Functions in Lp

A fundamental question in functional analysis concerns which subsets are dense in a given space. For Lp spaces, the answer reveals deep connections between regularity and approximation.

Theorem 9.9 (Density of Continuous Functions). Let X be a locally compact metric space and μ a regular Borel measure on X. For 1 ≤ p < ∞, the space Cc(X) of continuous functions with compact support is dense in Lp(X, μ).

Proof Sketch. The proof proceeds in three steps:

  1. Simple functions are dense in Lp: For any f ∈ Lp, we can approximate f by simple functions sₙ = Σᵢ aᵢ χAᵢ where each Aᵢ has finite measure. The dominated convergence theorem ensures ||f - sₙ||p → 0.

  2. Characteristic functions of measurable sets can be approximated by continuous functions: Given a measurable set A with finite measure, for any ε > 0 there exist a compact set K ⊆ A and an open set U ⊇ A such that μ(U  K) < ε. Using Urysohn’s lemma, we construct continuous functions that approximate χA.

  3. Combining the approximations: Linear combinations of the approximations in steps 1 and 2 yield continuous functions with compact support that approximate any Lp function. □

This theorem is crucial for both theoretical and computational purposes. It shows that despite the analytical complexity of Lp spaces, any function can be approximated by smooth, well-behaved functions. In numerical analysis, this justifies finite element methods and other approximation schemes.

The result fails for p = ∞, where continuous functions are not dense in L∞. This reflects the fact that L∞ contains essentially unbounded functions that cannot be uniformly approximated by continuous functions.

6.13 9.6 Dual Spaces and the Riesz Representation Theorem

The dual space of a normed space X, denoted X*, consists of all bounded linear functionals on X. Understanding dual spaces illuminates the geometric structure of function spaces and enables the formulation of weak solutions to differential equations.

For 1 < p < ∞ with conjugate exponent q (where 1/p + 1/q = 1), the dual space (Lp)* can be identified with Lq through the Riesz representation theorem.

Theorem 9.10 (Riesz Representation for Lp). Let 1 < p < ∞ and let q be its conjugate exponent. For every bounded linear functional T ∈ (Lp)*, there exists a unique g ∈ Lq such that

T(f) = ∫X f(x)g(x) dμ(x) for all f ∈ Lp

Moreover, ||T|| = ||g||q.

Proof Outline. Given T ∈ (Lp)*, we construct g through the Radon-Nikodym theorem. Define a signed measure ν by ν(A) = T(χA) for measurable sets A. The boundedness of T ensures ν is absolutely continuous with respect to μ, so ν = gμ for some g ∈ L1. The specific form of the bound ||T|| < ∞ forces g ∈ Lq with ||g||q = ||T||. □

For the Hilbert space L2, the situation is particularly elegant:

Theorem 9.11 (Riesz Representation for Hilbert Spaces). For every bounded linear functional T on a Hilbert space H, there exists a unique g ∈ H such that T(f) = ⟨f, g⟩ for all f ∈ H. Moreover, ||T|| = ||g||.

This theorem shows that (L2)* ≅ L2, making L2 self-dual. The identification is not merely algebraic but preserves the geometric structure through the inner product.

6.13.1 Example 9.5: A Linear Functional on L2

Consider the functional T: L2[0,1] → ℝ defined by T(f) = ∫₀¹ f(x)e^x dx.

By Hölder’s inequality with p = q = 2: |T(f)| = |∫₀¹ f(x)e^x dx| ≤ ||f||₂||e^x||₂ = ||f||₂(∫₀¹ e^(2x) dx)^(1/2) = ||f||₂((e² - 1)/2)^(1/2)

So T is bounded with ||T|| ≤ ((e² - 1)/2)^(1/2). By the Riesz representation theorem, T(f) = ⟨f, g⟩ where g(x) = e^x. Indeed:

T(f) = ∫₀¹ f(x)e^x dx = ⟨f, e^x⟩

using QuadGK, LinearAlgebra

# Verify the Riesz representation theorem numerically
function verify_riesz_representation()
    # Define the linear functional T(f) = ∫₀¹ f(x)e^x dx
    T(f) = quadgk(x -> f(x) * exp(x), 0, 1)[1]
    
    # The representing function is g(x) = e^x
    g(x) = exp(x)
    
    # Test with several functions in L²[0,1]
    test_functions = [
        x -> 1.0,              # constant function
        x -> x,                # linear function  
        x -> sin(π*x),         # sine function
        x -> x^2 - x,          # quadratic
    ]
    
    println("Verifying Riesz representation T(f) = ⟨f,g⟩:")
    println("Functional T(f) = ∫₀¹ f(x)eˣ dx")
    println("Representing function g(x) = eˣ")
    println()
    
    for (i, f) in enumerate(test_functions)
        # Compute T(f) directly
        T_f = T(f)
        
        # Compute ⟨f,g⟩ = ∫₀¹ f(x)g(x) dx  
        inner_product = quadgk(x -> f(x) * g(x), 0, 1)[1]
        
        println("Test function $i:")
        println("  T(f) = $(round(T_f, digits=6))")
        println("  ⟨f,g⟩ = $(round(inner_product, digits=6))")
        println("  Difference: $(round(abs(T_f - inner_product), digits=8))")
        println()
    end
    
    # Compute the norm of the functional
    g_norm = sqrt(quadgk(x -> g(x)^2, 0, 1)[1])
    println("||g||₂ = $(round(g_norm, digits=6))")
    println("Theoretical bound: ((e² - 1)/2)^(1/2) = $(round(sqrt((exp(2) - 1)/2), digits=6))")
end

verify_riesz_representation()

6.14 9.7 Applications to Modeling

The abstract theory of Lp spaces finds concrete application across many modeling domains. We highlight three fundamental connections that motivate the study of these function spaces.

6.14.1 Weak Solutions of PDEs and Sobolev Spaces

Classical solutions to partial differential equations require functions to be differentiable in the pointwise sense. However, many physically meaningful problems admit solutions that are not classically differentiable but possess “weak derivatives” in the sense of distributions.

Sobolev spaces Wᵏ·ᵖ combine Lp spaces with weak differentiability requirements:

Wᵏ·ᵖ(Ω) = {f ∈ Lp(Ω) : Dᵅf ∈ Lp(Ω) for all |α| ≤ k}

where Dᵅf denotes the weak partial derivative of order α. The space W¹·²(Ω) = H¹(Ω) is particularly important, as it naturally arises in variational formulations of elliptic PDEs.

For example, the weak form

7 Chapter 10: Fixed Point Theorems and Applications to Modeling

Fixed point theory provides fundamental tools for analyzing the existence and uniqueness of solutions to equations of the form \(x = f(x)\). Such equations arise naturally throughout mathematics and its applications: as steady states in dynamical systems, equilibrium points in economic models, solutions to differential equations, and convergence points of iterative algorithms. This chapter develops the core theorems of fixed point theory and demonstrates their power in establishing existence results that are central to mathematical modeling.

The notion of a fixed point is deceptively simple: given a function \(f: X \to X\), a fixed point is an element \(x^* \in X\) such that \(f(x^*) = x^*\). Yet this simple concept underlies some of the most important existence theorems in analysis. We begin with the Banach fixed point theorem, whose constructive proof provides both existence and a method for approximation. We then explore topological fixed point theorems that guarantee existence under more general conditions, though without providing explicit construction methods.

7.1 The Banach Fixed Point Theorem

The Banach fixed point theorem, also known as the contraction mapping principle, is perhaps the most practically useful fixed point theorem due to its constructive nature and strong convergence guarantees.

Definition 10.1 (Contraction Mapping). Let \((X, d)\) be a metric space. A function \(f: X \to X\) is called a contraction mapping if there exists a constant \(0 \leq k < 1\) such that \[d(f(x), f(y)) \leq k \cdot d(x, y)\] for all \(x, y \in X\). The constant \(k\) is called the contraction factor.

Note that every contraction mapping is uniformly continuous, since we can take \(\delta = \epsilon/k\) for any \(\epsilon > 0\).

Theorem 10.2 (Banach Fixed Point Theorem). Let \((X, d)\) be a complete metric space and let \(f: X \to X\) be a contraction mapping with contraction factor \(k\). Then: 1. \(f\) has a unique fixed point \(x^* \in X\). 2. For any starting point \(x_0 \in X\), the sequence \((x_n)\) defined by \(x_{n+1} = f(x_n)\) converges to \(x^*\). 3. The convergence is geometric: \(d(x_n, x^*) \leq k^n \cdot d(x_0, x^*)\). 4. An a priori error estimate: \(d(x_n, x^*) \leq \frac{k^n}{1-k} \cdot d(x_0, x_1)\). 5. An a posteriori error estimate: \(d(x_n, x^*) \leq \frac{k}{1-k} \cdot d(x_{n-1}, x_n)\).

Proof. We establish each claim systematically.

Existence via Convergence: Let \(x_0 \in X\) be arbitrary and define the sequence \((x_n)\) by \(x_{n+1} = f(x_n)\). We show this is a Cauchy sequence. For \(m > n\):

\[d(x_n, x_m) \leq d(x_n, x_{n+1}) + d(x_{n+1}, x_{n+2}) + \cdots + d(x_{m-1}, x_m)\]

Since \(d(x_{j+1}, x_{j+2}) = d(f(x_j), f(x_{j+1})) \leq k \cdot d(x_j, x_{j+1})\), we have:

\[d(x_n, x_m) \leq d(x_n, x_{n+1})(1 + k + k^2 + \cdots + k^{m-n-1}) \leq d(x_n, x_{n+1}) \frac{1}{1-k}\]

By induction, \(d(x_n, x_{n+1}) \leq k^n d(x_0, x_1)\), so:

\[d(x_n, x_m) \leq \frac{k^n}{1-k} d(x_0, x_1)\]

This approaches zero as \(n \to \infty\), proving \((x_n)\) is Cauchy. Since \(X\) is complete, \((x_n)\) converges to some \(x^* \in X\).

Fixed Point Property: Since \(f\) is continuous and \(x_{n+1} = f(x_n)\), taking limits gives \(x^* = f(x^*)\).

Uniqueness: If \(x^*\) and \(y^*\) are both fixed points, then: \[d(x^*, y^*) = d(f(x^*), f(y^*)) \leq k \cdot d(x^*, y^*)\]

Since \(k < 1\), this implies \(d(x^*, y^*) = 0\), so \(x^* = y^*\).

Error Estimates: The geometric convergence follows from \(d(x_n, x_{n+1}) \leq k^n d(x_0, x_1)\) and the triangle inequality. The a posteriori estimate follows by taking \(m \to \infty\) in our Cauchy sequence analysis. □

7.1.1 Computational Implementation

using LinearAlgebra

"""
    banach_iteration(f, x0, k; tol=1e-8, maxiter=1000)

Implement Banach fixed point iteration with error estimates.
Returns (x_star, iterations, error_estimates).
"""
function banach_iteration(f, x0, k; tol=1e-8, maxiter=1000)
    x_curr = copy(x0)
    error_estimates = Float64[]
    
    for i in 1:maxiter
        x_next = f(x_curr)
        dist_curr = norm(x_next - x_curr)
        
        # A posteriori error estimate
        error_est = (k / (1 - k)) * dist_curr
        push!(error_estimates, error_est)
        
        if error_est < tol
            return x_next, i, error_estimates
        end
        
        x_curr = x_next
    end
    
    @warn "Maximum iterations reached without convergence"
    return x_curr, maxiter, error_estimates
end

# Example: Find fixed point of f(x) = 0.5*cos(x) + 0.25
function example_contraction()
    f(x) = 0.5 * cos(x) + 0.25
    k = 0.5  # |f'(x)| = 0.5|sin(x)| ≤ 0.5
    
    x_star, iters, errors = banach_iteration(f, 1.0, k)
    
    println("Fixed point: x* = $x_star")
    println("Verification: f(x*) = $(f(x_star))")
    println("Converged in $iters iterations")
    println("Final error estimate: $(errors[end])")
    
    return x_star, iters, errors
end

example_contraction()

7.2 Application to Ordinary Differential Equations

The Banach fixed point theorem provides a powerful method for proving existence and uniqueness of solutions to initial value problems. This approach, known as the Picard iteration method, transforms the differential equation into an equivalent integral equation with a contraction mapping.

Theorem 10.3 (Picard-Lindelöf Theorem). Consider the initial value problem \[\frac{dy}{dt} = f(t, y), \quad y(t_0) = y_0\] where \(f: [t_0 - a, t_0 + a] \times [y_0 - b, y_0 + b] \to \mathbb{R}\) is continuous and satisfies the Lipschitz condition \[|f(t, y_1) - f(t, y_2)| \leq L |y_1 - y_2|\] for some constant \(L > 0\). Then there exists \(h > 0\) such that the problem has a unique solution on \([t_0 - h, t_0 + h]\).

Proof. We transform the differential equation into the integral equation: \[y(t) = y_0 + \int_{t_0}^t f(s, y(s)) ds\]

Define the operator \(T\) on the space \(C([t_0 - h, t_0 + h])\) by: \[(Ty)(t) = y_0 + \int_{t_0}^t f(s, y(s)) ds\]

We must choose \(h\) such that \(T\) maps a suitable closed subset of this space into itself and is a contraction.

Let \(M = \max_{|t-t_0| \leq a, |y-y_0| \leq b} |f(t,y)|\) and define: \[X = \{y \in C([t_0-h, t_0+h]) : \|y - y_0\|_\infty \leq b\}\]

For \(h = \min\{a, b/M, 1/(2L)\}\), one can verify that \(T\) maps \(X\) into itself with contraction factor \(k = Lh \leq 1/2 < 1\). The Banach fixed point theorem then guarantees a unique solution. □

7.2.1 Numerical Implementation of Picard Iteration

using QuadGK

"""
    picard_iteration(f, t0, y0, t_span, n_terms=5)

Compute Picard iterates for the IVP y' = f(t,y), y(t0) = y0.
"""
function picard_iteration(f, t0, y0, t_span, n_terms=5)
    # Initialize with y0(t) = y0 (constant function)
    iterates = [t -> y0]
    
    for k in 1:n_terms
        y_prev = iterates[end]
        
        # Define next iterate: y_{k+1}(t) = y0 + ∫_{t0}^t f(s, y_k(s)) ds
        y_next = function(t)
            if t == t0
                return y0
            else
                integrand(s) = f(s, y_prev(s))
                integral, _ = quadgk(integrand, t0, t)
                return y0 + integral
            end
        end
        
        push!(iterates, y_next)
    end
    
    return iterates
end

# Example: y' = -2ty, y(0) = 1 (exact solution: y = exp(-t²))
function picard_example()
    f(t, y) = -2 * t * y
    t0, y0 = 0.0, 1.0
    
    iterates = picard_iteration(f, t0, y0, (0.0, 1.0), 4)
    
    # Evaluate iterates at t = 0.5
    t_test = 0.5
    exact_value = exp(-t_test^2)
    
    println("Picard iterates at t = $t_test:")
    for (i, y_k) in enumerate(iterates)
        approx_value = y_k(t_test)
        error = abs(approx_value - exact_value)
        println("y_$(i-1)($t_test) = $(round(approx_value, digits=6)), error = $(round(error, digits=8))")
    end
    
    println("Exact value: $(round(exact_value, digits=6))")
    return iterates
end

picard_example()

7.3 Topological Fixed Point Theorems

While the Banach fixed point theorem provides strong constructive results, it requires the restrictive contraction condition. Topological fixed point theorems relax this requirement at the cost of losing the constructive proof and uniqueness guarantee.

Theorem 10.4 (Brouwer Fixed Point Theorem). Every continuous function \(f: D^n \to D^n\) from the closed unit ball in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) to itself has at least one fixed point.

The proof of Brouwer’s theorem requires topological techniques beyond our scope, but we can understand its geometric intuition: continuously deforming the unit ball cannot avoid leaving some point fixed.

Corollary 10.5. Every continuous function \(f: [0,1] \to [0,1]\) has a fixed point.

Theorem 10.6 (Schauder Fixed Point Theorem). Let \(X\) be a Banach space, \(K \subset X\) a nonempty, closed, bounded, and convex subset, and \(f: K \to K\) a compact operator (i.e., \(f\) maps bounded sets to relatively compact sets). Then \(f\) has at least one fixed point.

The Schauder theorem extends Brouwer’s result to infinite-dimensional spaces by requiring compactness of the mapping rather than finite dimension of the domain.

7.4 Applications to Economic Equilibria

Fixed point theorems provide the mathematical foundation for proving the existence of economic equilibria. The fundamental insight is that equilibrium conditions can be formulated as fixed point problems.

Example 10.7 (Nash Equilibrium Existence). Consider a finite strategic game with \(n\) players, where player \(i\) has strategy set \(S_i\) (a compact convex subset of \(\mathbb{R}^{k_i}\)) and payoff function \(u_i: S_1 \times \cdots \times S_n \to \mathbb{R}\) that is continuous and concave in player \(i\)’s strategy.

Define the best response correspondence \(BR_i: S_{-i} \to S_i\) where \(S_{-i} = \prod_{j \neq i} S_j\) by: \[BR_i(s_{-i}) = \arg\max_{s_i \in S_i} u_i(s_i, s_{-i})\]

A Nash equilibrium is a strategy profile \(s^* = (s_1^*, \ldots, s_n^*)\) such that \(s_i^* \in BR_i(s_{-i}^*)\) for all \(i\).

By constructing a suitable continuous function \(f: S \to S\) where \(S = S_1 \times \cdots \times S_n\) whose fixed points correspond to Nash equilibria, and applying Brouwer’s theorem to the compact convex set \(S\), one can prove that every finite game with these properties has at least one Nash equilibrium.

7.4.1 Computational Nash Equilibrium

using Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL

"""
    find_nash_equilibrium_2player(payoff1, payoff2, n_strategies=10)

Find Nash equilibrium for 2-player game using fixed point iteration
on discretized mixed strategy space.
"""
function find_nash_equilibrium_2player(payoff1, payoff2, n_strategies=10)
    # Discretize strategy spaces
    strategies = range(0, 1, length=n_strategies)
    
    # Define best response functions
    function best_response_1(p2_mixed)
        # Expected payoffs for pure strategies against mixed strategy p2_mixed
        expected_payoffs = [sum(payoff1[i, j] * p2_mixed[j] for j in 1:n_strategies) 
                          for i in 1:n_strategies]
        
        # Return probability distribution over best responses
        max_payoff = maximum(expected_payoffs)
        best_responses = expected_payoffs .≈ max_payoff
        return best_responses / sum(best_responses)
    end
    
    function best_response_2(p1_mixed)
        expected_payoffs = [sum(payoff2[i, j] * p1_mixed[i] for i in 1:n_strategies) 
                          for j in 1:n_strategies]
        max_payoff = maximum(expected_payoffs)
        best_responses = expected_payoffs .≈ max_payoff
        return best_responses / sum(best_responses)
    end
    
    # Fixed point iteration
    p1 = ones(n_strategies) / n_strategies  # Initial uniform mixed strategy
    p2 = ones(n_strategies) / n_strategies
    
    for iter in 1:100
        p1_new = best_response_1(p2)
        p2_new = best_response_2(p1)
        
        if norm(p1_new - p1) + norm(p2_new - p2) < 1e-6
            println("Converged in $iter iterations")
            return p1_new, p2_new
        end
        
        # Damped iteration for stability
        α = 0.5
        p1 = α * p1_new + (1-α) * p1
        p2 = α * p2_new + (1-α) * p2
    end
    
    @warn "Did not converge"
    return p1, p2
end

# Example: Prisoner's Dilemma
function prisoners_dilemma_example()
    # Payoff matrices: rows = player 1 strategies, cols = player 2 strategies
    # Strategy 1: Cooperate, Strategy 2: Defect
    payoff1 = [3.0 0.0; 5.0 1.0]  # Player 1's payoffs
    payoff2 = [3.0 5.0; 0.0 1.0]  # Player 2's payoffs
    
    p1_eq, p2_eq = find_nash_equilibrium_2player(payoff1, payoff2, 2)
    
    println("Nash equilibrium mixed strategies:")
    println("Player 1: Cooperate with probability $(round(p1_eq[1], digits=3))")
    println("Player 2: Cooperate with probability $(round(p2_eq[1], digits=3))")
    
    return p1_eq, p2_eq
end

prisoners_dilemma_example()

7.5 Population Dynamics and Steady States

Fixed point theorems are essential for analyzing steady states in population models. Consider a general population model with state vector \(x(t) \in \mathbb{R}_+^n\) representing population densities and dynamics \(\dot{x} = F(x)\).

Definition 10.8 (Steady State). A steady state or equilibrium of the dynamical system \(\dot{x} = F(x)\) is a point \(x^*\) such that \(F(x^*) = 0\), equivalently a fixed point of \(x \mapsto x + \tau F(x)\) for any \(\tau > 0\).

7.5.1 The Lotka-Volterra Competition Model

Consider the two-species competition model: \[\begin{align} \dot{x}_1 &= r_1 x_1 \left(1 - \frac{x_1}{K_1} - \alpha_{12} \frac{x_2}{K_1}\right) \\ \dot{x}_2 &= r_2 x_2 \left(1 - \frac{x_2}{K_2} - \alpha_{21} \frac{x_1}{K_2}\right) \end{align}\]

The fixed points satisfy: \[x_1 = x_1 \left(1 - \frac{x_1}{K_1} - \alpha_{12} \frac{x_2}{K_1}\right), \quad x_2 = x_2 \left(1 - \frac{x_2}{K_2} - \alpha_{21} \frac{x_1}{K_2}\right)\]

Beyond the trivial solutions, the nontrivial steady states solve the linear system: \[\frac{x_1}{K_1} + \alpha_{12} \frac{x_2}{K_1} = 1, \quad \alpha_{21} \frac{x_1}{K_2} + \frac{x_2}{K_2} = 1\]

using DifferentialEquations, Plots

"""
    lotka_volterra_competition(du, u, p, t)

Lotka-Volterra competition model dynamics.
"""
function lotka_volterra_competition!(du, u, p, t)
    x1, x2 = u
    r1, r2, K1, K2, α12, α21 = p
    
    du[1] = r1 * x1 * (1 - x1/K1 - α12 * x2/K1)
    du[2] = r2 * x2 * (1 - x2/K2 - α21 * x1/K2)
end

"""
    find_competition_equilibria(r1, r2, K1, K2, α12, α21)

Find all equilibria of the competition model analytically.
"""
function find_competition_equilibria(r1, r2, K1, K2, α12, α21)
    equilibria = Vector{Vector{Float64}}()
    
    # Trivial equilibrium: extinction
    push!(equilibria, [0.0, 0.0])
    
    # Boundary equilibria
    push!(equilibria, [K1, 0.0])  # Species 2 extinct
    push!(equilibria, [0.0, K2])  # Species 1 extinct
    
    # Interior equilibrium (if it exists and is positive)
    det_A = 1 - α12 * α21
    if abs(det_A) > 1e-10  # Non-singular system
        x1_star = (K1 - α12 * K2) / det_A
        x2_star = (K2 - α21 * K1) / det_A
        
        if x1_star > 0 && x2_star > 0
            push!(equilibria, [x1_star, x2_star])
            println("Interior equilibrium exists: x1* = $(round(x1_star, digits=3)), x2* = $(round(x2_star, digits=3))")
        else
            println("Interior equilibrium is not biologically feasible")
        end
    else
        println("Competitive exclusion case: α12 * α21 = 1")
    end
    
    return equilibria
end

# Example: Analyze competition dynamics
function competition_analysis()
    # Parameters: moderate competition
    r1, r2 = 1.0, 0.8
    K1, K2 = 100.0, 80.0
    α12, α21 = 0.6, 0.7  # Competition coefficients
    
    params = [r1, r2, K1, K2, α12, α21]
    
    # Find equilibria
    equilibria = find_competition_equilibria(r1, r2, K1, K2, α12, α21)
    
    # Simulate trajectories from different initial conditions
    prob = ODEProblem(lotka_volterra_competition!, [20.0, 30.0], (0.0, 20.0), params)
    sol = solve(prob, Tsit5())
    
    println("Trajectory endpoints: x1 = $(round(sol[end][1], digits=2)), x2 = $(round(sol[end][2], digits=2))")
    
    return equilibria, sol
end

equilibria, solution = competition_analysis()

7.6 Iterative Numerical Methods

Fixed point theory provides the theoretical foundation for analyzing convergence of iterative numerical methods. Many classical algorithms can be formulated as fixed point iterations.

7.6.1 Linear System Solvers

Consider solving the linear system \(Ax = b\) where \(A\) is nonsingular. We can rewrite this as a fixed point problem \(x = (I - A)x + b\) or more generally \(x = Mx + c\) where \(M = I - P^{-1}A\) and \(c = P^{-1}b\) for some preconditioner \(P\).

Theorem 10.9 (Convergence of Linear Iterative Methods). The iteration \(x^{(k+1)} = Mx^{(k)} + c\) converges to the unique solution of \(Ax = b\) for any initial guess \(x^{(0)}\) if and only if the spectral radius \(\rho(M) < 1\).

Proof. The error \(e^{(k)} = x^{(k)} - x^*\) satisfies \(e^{(k+1)} = Me^{(k)}\), so \(e^{(k)} = M^k e^{(0)}\). Convergence for all initial errors occurs if and only if \(M^k \to 0\) as \(k \to \infty\), which is equivalent to \(\rho(M) < 1\). □

7.6.2 Implementation of Classical Iterative Methods

using LinearAlgebra, SparseArrays

"""
    jacobi_iteration(A, b, x0; tol=1e-8, maxiter=1000)

Solve Ax = b using Jacobi iteration.
"""
function jacobi_iteration(A, b, x0; tol=1e-8, maxiter=1000)
    n = length(b)
    x = copy(x0)
    x_new = similar(x)
    
    # Extract diagonal and off-diagonal parts
    D = Diagonal(diag(A))
    R = A - D
    
    # Check if diagonal elements are nonzero
    if any(abs.(diag(D)) .< 1e-14)
        error("Matrix has zero diagonal elements")
    end
    
    residuals = Float64[]
    
    for k in 1:maxiter
        # x^{(k+1)} = D^{-1}(b - R*x^{(k)})
        x_new .= (b - R * x) ./ diag(D)
        
        residual = norm(A * x_new - b)
        push!(residuals, residual)
        
        if residual < tol
            println("Jacobi converged in $k iterations")
            return x_new, k, residuals
        end
        
        x, x_new = x_new, x
    end
    
    @warn "Jacobi method did not converge"
    return x, maxiter, residuals
end

"""
    gauss_seidel_iteration(A, b, x0; tol=1e-8, maxiter=1000)

Solve Ax = b using Gauss-Seidel iteration.
"""
function gauss_seidel_iteration(A, b, x0; tol=1e-8, maxiter=1000)
    n = length(b)
    x = copy(x0)
    residuals = Float64[]
    
    for k in 1:maxiter
        # Update components sequentially
        for i in 1:n
            sigma = sum(A[i,j] * x[j] for j in 1:n if j != i)
            x[i] = (b[i] - sigma) / A[i,i]
        end
        
        residual = norm(A * x - b)
        push!(residuals, residual)
        
        if residual < tol
            println("Gauss-Seidel converged in $k iterations")
            return x, k, residuals
        end
    end
    
    @warn "Gauss-Seidel method did not converge"
    return x, maxiter, residuals
end

# Compare methods on a test system
function compare_iterative_methods()
    # Create a diagonally dominant system (ensures convergence)
    n = 50
    A = randn(n, n)
    A = A + (n + 1) * I  # Make diagonally dominant
    x_true = randn(n)
    b = A * x_true
    
    x0 = zeros(n)  # Zero initial guess
    
    # Solve with both methods
    x_jacobi, iter_j, res_j = jacobi_iteration(A, b, x0)
    x_gs, iter_gs, res_gs = gauss_seidel_iteration(A, b, x0)
    
    println("True solution error (Jacobi): $(norm(x_jacobi - x_true))")
    println("True solution error (Gauss-Seidel): $(norm(x_gs - x_true))")
    
    # Analyze spectral radius for Jacobi method
    D = Diagonal(diag(A))
    R = A - D
    M_jacobi = -D \ R
    ρ_jacobi = maximum(abs.(eigvals(M_jacobi)))
    
    println("Jacobi iteration matrix spectral radius: $(round(ρ_jacobi, digits=4))")
    
    return (x_jacobi, iter_j, res_j), (x_gs, iter_gs, res_gs)
end

jacobi_result, gs_result = compare_iterative_methods()

7.7 Advanced Applications in Dynamical Systems

Fixed points play a central role in understanding the long-term behavior of dynamical systems. Beyond their role as equilibrium states, the stability properties of fixed points determine the qualitative dynamics of nonlinear systems.

Definition 10.10 (Stability of Fixed Points). Let \(x^*\) be a fixed point of the dynamical system \(\dot{x} = F(x)\). The fixed point is: - Stable if for every \(\epsilon > 0\), there exists \(\delta > 0\) such that \(\|x(0) - x^*\| < \delta\) implies \(\|x(t) - x^*\| < \epsilon\) for all \(t \geq 0\). - Asymptotically stable if it is stable and there exists \(\delta > 0\) such that \(\|x(0) - x^*\| < \delta\) implies \(\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t) = x^*\).

The linearization theorem connects local stability to the eigenvalues of the Jacobian matrix at the fixed point.

Theorem 10.11 (Linearization Theorem). Let \(x^*\) be a fixed point of \(\dot{x} = F(x)\) where \(F\) is \(C^1\). If all eigenvalues of \(DF(x^*)\) have negative real parts, then \(x^*\) is asymptotically stable. If any eigenvalue has positive real part, then \(x^*\) is unstable.

7.7.1 Predator-Prey Dynamics

```julia ““” predator_prey!(du, u, p, t)

Lotka-Volterra predator-prey model with logistic growth for prey. ““” function predator_prey!(du, u, p, t) x, y = u # prey, predator populations r, K, a, b, c, d = p

du[1] = r * x * (1 - x/K) - a * x * y  # prey dynamics
du[2] = b * a * x * y - c * y           # predator dynamics

end

““” analyze_predator_prey_equilibria(r, K, a, b, c, d)

Find and analyze stability of equilibria in predator-prey model. ““” function analyze_predator_prey_equilibria(r, K, a, b, c, d) println(“Predator-Prey Model Analysis”) println(“============================”)

# Trivial equilibrium: no species
eq1 = [0.0, 0.0]
println("Equilibrium 1: $(eq1)")

# Prey-only equilibrium
eq2 = [K, 0.0]
println("Equilibrium 2: $(eq2)")

# Coexistence equilibrium (if positive)
x_star = c / (b * a)
y_star = (r * (1 - x_star/K)) / a

if x_star > 0 && x_star < K && y_star > 0
    eq3 = [x_star, y_star]
    println("Coexistence equilibrium: $(round.(eq3, digits=3))")
    
    # Stability analysis via Jacobian
    J11 = r * (1 - 2*x_star/K) - a * y_star
    J12 = -a * x_star
    J21 = b * a * y_star
    J22 = b * a * x_star - c
    
    J = [J11 J12; J21 J22]
